rto_tiers: Operational capability within 12 hours of activation, sustained up to 30 days (90 days for pandemic flu); implementation timeline appendix sequences actions at 0:00/2:00/4:00/8:00 hours and within 1 day / 3 days; 'business disruption' trigger = loss of an instructional component or any critical function for 2 consecutive instructional days or 3 consecutive calendar days
succession_depth: Two named alternates deep per key position (Appendix A), plus a Cabinet succession line; President -> Alternate 1 -> Alternate 2 with departmental successions
vital_records_approach: Off-site 'GO KIT' with hardcopy Business Process Guides, vital-records list and computer access pathways, essential electronic files on flash/CD; PeopleSoft vital records on Salt Lake City server with annual Chancellor's Office backup exercise; facilities blueprints/as-builts on-site
tte_cadence: Every business unit performs at least one tabletop exercise per year (each sub-unit within 7 years); not less than every seventh year a full-scale mock exercise from emergency response through BCP; FEMA IS-546/547/548/520/522 courses recommended; after-action reports within 30/90 days
devolution_treatment: Listed as a consolidated continuity element and named as an operational endpoint ('sustained operations for up to 30 days... or until devolution'), though devolution mechanics are lighter than the named alternate-site/relocation procedures
reconstitution_treatment: Dedicated 'How Will CSUB Return to Normal?' section: BCP IC plus Chief of UPD and Response Team determine completion; President in collaboration with Cabinet sets conditions for resuming normal operations; after-action report within 30 days
academic_continuity_treatment: Class relocation, modified syllabi/schedules, web-based classes, Deans Council convened for alternate teaching protocols; Class 3 (communicable disease) recovery includes alternate instruction delivery; Provost confers with Chancellor's Office and faculty on course delivery and academic credit
it_continuity_treatment: Separate IT Recovery Plan and Kuali plan referenced throughout; clustered servers/redundant VMs, contingent contracts with CENIC/Dell/Verizon/PBX vendors, Data Center Disaster Recovery Plan, automatic Chancellor's Office web failover, mobile trailer/terminal reconnection of the My CSUB portal within 24 hours
interdependencies_treatment: Three incident classes scaled by impact on interdependencies; contingent vendor contracts enumerated; mutual aid and cooperative agreements with other CSU campuses (e.g., payroll input at CSU Fresno) and local agencies/hospitals

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly maps the plan to the consolidated COOP/COG continuity-program elements drawn from the Cal EMA template
unusual_decisions.quote: A viable Business Continuity Plan identifies essential functions and consists of plans and procedures, alternate facilities, interoperable communications, data support systems, and is reinforced by comprehensive training, orientation, and exercise programs.

unusual_decisions.decision: Pre-positions contingent contracts for tents, water, generators, sanitation, food and trash so the campus can stand up a temporary tented operating site for up to 30 days
unusual_decisions.quote: Contingent contracts (vendors listed below) may be utilized during an event to procure items such as temporary tents, sanitation facilities, water, food, fencing, generators, trash removal, and computers. These contracts provide the campus with the ability to sustain operations for a period of up to 30 days.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines a precise quantitative activation trigger tied to instructional/critical-function downtime
unusual_decisions.quote: A "business disruption" is defined as the loss or anticipated loss of a) any instructional component, or b) any critical function for two consecutive instructional days or three consecutive calendar days.

analyst_notes: Read all 48 pages. CSUB's BCP is the most complete plan in this batch, explicitly structured around the consolidated continuity-of-operations/continuity-of-government elements from the Cal EMA template and authorized under CSU EO 1014, EO 921, HSPD-20/NSPD-51, and NIMS. It covers all 22 benchmark components: signed-policy authority, three-incident-class CONOPS, BIA/risk assessment, essential functions with 12h/30d/90d objectives, two-deep succession (Appendix A) and a delegations-of-authority table with triggers/limits, multiple alternate-facility models (reciprocal CSU campuses, tented sites, telework), off-site GO-KIT vital records, a standalone IT Recovery/Data Center DR plan, academic continuity, devolution, a return-to-normal reconstitution section, and a tiered TT&E program (annual tabletops, septennial full-scale mock, FEMA courses, AARs).

essential_functions: EF#1 University Leadership (President, Provost, VP Administration & Finance) - RTO 4 hours
EF#2 Public Safety - continuous (no recovery time)
EF#3 Communications (alert notification, police dispatch, executive/media) - tools RTO 30 min / content RTO 4 hours
EF#4 Basic Services (food, emergency maintenance, access control, transportation) - RTO 6-12 hours
EF#5 Fiscal Operations and Functional Processes (payroll, purchasing, contracts, critical data) - RTO 3 hours
EF#6 Academic Continuity (instruction, research, space, pivot to virtual) - RTO 3 days, some research continuous
rto_tiers: Per-Mission-Essential-Function recovery times (30 minutes to 3 days); operational capability within 12 hours of activation; academic classes reinstated within 2 weeks; sustained operations up to 30 days
succession_depth: At least three positions deep where possible; orders-of-succession tables for seven offices (President, Academic Affairs, Administration & Finance, Advancement & University Relations, Enrollment Management, Human Resources, Student Success & Student Life), described by position/title and including devolution counterparts
vital_records_approach: Extensive Essential Records section keyed to Texas Government Code 441.180 'vital State record'; records digitized and accessible virtually, IT responsible for security/recovery, hard copies scanned to network/portable drive; access available at continuity facility within 12 hours; BIA and insurance policies designated essential records
tte_cadence: Dedicated Test, Training, Exercises section; colleges/divisions/departments train on their COOP and exercise it annually, submitting documentation to the Continuity Coordinator by April 1; After-Action Review, Corrective Action Plan, and remedial action plan; successors and continuity personnel participate
devolution_treatment: Dedicated Devolution of Control and Direction section: transfers statutory authority and responsibility for essential functions from UHD's primary staff/facilities to a University of Houston System campus's employees/facilities if leadership/staff are unavailable, sustained for an extended period
reconstitution_treatment: Phase 4 Reconstitution in the CONOPS plus a dedicated Reconstitution section; surviving/replacement personnel resume normal operations; redeployment plans for return to the primary facility
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Continuity is Mission Essential Function #6 (maintain instruction, research, and space; transition to a virtual environment when campus is untenable; RTO 3 days, some research continuous); plan targets reinstating classes within 2 weeks
it_continuity_treatment: Supplemented by an extensive IT COOP and Disaster Recovery Plan maintained by the AVP of IT (an essential record); IT responsible for security/recovery of vital state records in digital format; computer systems backed up regularly with access available at the Emergency Relocation Site
interdependencies_treatment: Departments report dependencies and business processes via the BIA process; assumptions detail reliance on UH System resources, utilities, telecom, financial/public-health/security/transportation services; coordination with HDMD, City of Houston, Harris County

unusual_decisions.decision: Uses reciprocal University of Houston System campuses as the Emergency Relocation Site and as the devolution destination, providing classrooms/labs/library/computing within 12 hours
unusual_decisions.quote: If UHD is unable to deploy an ERG to an ERS due to loss of personnel, UHD will devolve to one of the University of Houston System campuses.

unusual_decisions.decision: Sets distinct recovery-time objectives for communications tools versus communications content
unusual_decisions.quote: The recovery time objective for the tools needed to communicate is 30 minutes while the recovery time objective for the content needed to communicate is four (4) hours.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates the key personnel relocated under the plan as a named Emergency Relocation Group operating from an Emergency Relocation Site or virtual environment
unusual_decisions.quote: Key personnel relocated under this plan are collectively known as the UHD Emergency Relocation Group (ERG). Upon plan activation, these members may move to a virtual environment or deploy to a continuity facility and establish operational capability to perform essential functions within 12 hours from the time of plan activation up to a 30-day period or until normal operations can be resumed.

analyst_notes: NOTE: the supplied 2023 URL returned HTTP 404; I coded UHD's current 2025-2026 COOP (same institution, same full-coop document type) fetched from uhd.edu (2025-uhd-coop-campus.pdf). Read all 55 pages. This is a complete FCD-element COOP: signed promulgation statement, four-phase CONOPS (Readiness/Activation/Operations/Reconstitution), six Mission Essential Functions with explicit RTOs, three-deep succession tables across seven offices, delegations of authority with triggers/limits, a robust Essential Records section, alternate ERS facilities plus telework, interoperable communications, a dedicated devolution section, a budgeting & acquisition section, a TT&E program with AAR/CAP, and citations to FEMA's Continuity Guidance Circular, PPD-40/NSPD-51 National Continuity Policy, and Texas statutes. All 22 components present.

essential_functions: Essential/critical campus departments identified via BIA as top priority to restore after a disaster (top-down campus-wide BCP with departmental BCPs as building blocks); critical functions defined as those whose loss within a specified time would cause unmanageable curtailment/suspension/termination of University operations
rto_tiers: Predetermined recovery time objectives referenced (BIA identifies priority of restoration and recovery requirements); activation flowchart uses 0-24 hrs and 0-48 hrs decision windows; no published numeric tier table in the master plan
succession_depth: President succession 9 positions deep via Presidential Directive 2009-03: President; Provost/VP Academic Affairs; VP Administration & Finance/CFO; VP Student Affairs; VP University Advancement; President's Chief of Staff; (for major disasters) Vice Provost for Academic Planning & Budgets; Chief of Police; VP IT/CIO
vital_records_approach: Records Retention Policy (BCP Team manages records per CSU policy and law; Records Retention Schedule); BCP manual stored offsite with offsite data backup storage media location, insurance contracts and means to reestablish physical records
tte_cadence: Section 4 Testing and Validation (with treatment of test failures); ongoing maintenance testing - oversight & remedial (ICQ 16): information update/testing, testing/validation of technical solutions, testing/verification of organization recovery procedures; Section 6 Business Continuity Training; plan re-evaluated after an exercise
devolution_treatment: Not addressed as devolution to a separate unit/site (succession + Emergency Relocation Site relocation cover related ground)
reconstitution_treatment: BCP Conditions of Termination: Emergency Director declares normal operations may resume when facilities/infrastructure/services are sustainable and reliable; activation flowchart includes 'Implement Reconstitution Plans' and 'Return to Normal Operations'
academic_continuity_treatment: First BCP Guiding Principle is Continuity of student learning: 'Ensure access to and consistent delivery of, academic education and related support services to students (existing and prospective) within the current and projected capacity of the University'
it_continuity_treatment: Disaster recovery infrastructure supporting work-process recovery within RTOs; testing and validation of technical solutions (virus definitions, technical deployments); offsite data backup storage; BCP manual includes technical requirements/readiness
interdependencies_treatment: BCP manual captures clients and vendors contact info, offsite data backup location, supply chain (means to establish a new supply chain); maintenance triggers include changes to important vendors/suppliers and their contact details

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats BCP as a phase distinct from crisis management, formally sequenced to begin only after campus stabilization
unusual_decisions.quote: Business Continuity: This phase involves restarting mission-critical (essential) business operations after a major disruption occurs.

unusual_decisions.decision: Hands BCP activation/operation authority to the Emergency Director rather than the BCP Coordinator, integrating BCP under emergency management command
unusual_decisions.quote: Upon the Emergency Director’s declaration of BCP activation, the BCP Coordinator coordinates BCP operation with the EOC Section Chiefs and the Emergency Management Recovery Team (Recovery Manager and Recovery Coordinators).

unusual_decisions.decision: Runs the program through a monthly Business Continuity Steering Committee that reviews, prioritizes, assigns and signs off on BCP tasks division-by-division
unusual_decisions.quote: BCSC is scheduled to meet once a month. These meetings are designed to be worksessions

analyst_notes: A genuinely comprehensive Business Continuity Planning Master Plan (v3.0) under CSU EO 1014, with a clear authority/delegation chain (VP Admin & Finance delegated by the President; Presidential Directive 2009-03 nine-deep succession), a full CONOPS (activation/operation/termination phases plus a COOP/ERS/reconstitution flowchart), three formal analyses (BIA, Risk/Vulnerabilities, Threat), a monthly Steering Committee, records retention, offsite backup, secondary work sites, testing/validation with treatment of failures, training, and a maintenance cycle. It satisfies all benchmark components except an explicit devolution of essential functions to a separate organizational unit. One of the strongest documents in the batch.

essential_functions: Information Technology (network & data, telephone, IT services)
Facilities and Campus Services (transportation, facilities management)
Dean of Students (student & community crisis support)
rto_tiers: Essential services prioritized; Emergency Support Functions carry 'immediate recovery time objectives'; activation within 12 hours of a significant disruption, sustained up to 30 days; academic-suspension timing rules (first three weeks = semester lost; last three weeks = may be completed)
succession_depth: University Order of Succession three deep (President -> Provost -> Secretary of the Corporation -> CFO -> Dean of Weill Medical College), Table 6.1, defined by University bylaws
vital_records_approach: Section H Vital Files, Records, and Databases with a Vital Records Maintenance Strategy table (develop a process, identify vital records via the C-COOP tool, review protection needs); content not media determines criticality; CIT network security plan supports secure records
tte_cadence: Not established as a program; TT&E appears only in the COOP definition ('validate the capability through tests, training, and exercises') and as an after-action review/corrective action step during reconstitution; no training plan, exercise types, or recurring cadence
devolution_treatment: Not addressed (succession is provided but no devolution of essential functions to a separate unit/site)
reconstitution_treatment: Dedicated 'Reconstitution and Recovery' section: demobilize resources, supervise orderly return to normal/temporary/new facility, verify systems, conduct an after-action review and corrective/remedial action plan
academic_continuity_treatment: Detailed Academic Continuity section (alternative course delivery/distance learning, semester-timing rules for suspension, equitable treatment of students lacking technology) plus a separate Research Operations Continuity section (space/equipment documentation, inter-college sharing agreements, data/organism backup)
it_continuity_treatment: Section I Critical Business Applications and Technology Resources: connectivity and system access required at the continuity location, servers backed up frequently at more than one location, CIT maintains a disaster continuity and contingency plan for central service applications
interdependencies_treatment: Establishes communication with supporting and supported organizations and stakeholders; inter-college agreements to share space; coordination with CIT, Office of Space Planning, Registrar; C-COOP tool maps unit resource needs

unusual_decisions.decision: Provides a university-wide web-based continuity-planning tool (C-COOP) as the central repository for unit essential services, priorities, personnel/resource needs, and vital-records mapping
unusual_decisions.quote: To facilitate alignment with continuity of operations guiding principles, the University provides a robust, web-based software tool called C-COOP (www.coop.ehs.cornell.edu).

unusual_decisions.decision: Codifies academic-calendar decision rules for how much of a term is lost based on when instruction is suspended
unusual_decisions.quote: As a very general presumption, if the suspension of instruction occurs within the first three weeks of a term, the semester will be considered to be lost and will need to be repeated in its entirety. If the suspension of instruction occurs in the last three weeks of the term, the semester may be considered to be completed, subject to course-specific exceptions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats extraordinary preparation costs as a unit responsibility funded from unit reserves, with central funding only via the annual budget process
unusual_decisions.quote: Such costs will generally be considered a financial responsibility of the operating unit, to be funded out of current available resources including unit reserves... funding may be requested as part of the annual budget process to the university Operating Plans Committee.

analyst_notes: Read all 18 pages. Cornell's COOP v3.0 is an exemplary FCD-element plan: a concept of operations, NIMS/National Disaster Recovery Framework alignment with a formal Authorities and References appendix (NFPA 1600, EMAP, FEMA CPG 101/201, SUNY requirements) and a Policy 8.3 promulgation statement, essential services (ESF table), succession (Table 6.1, three deep), delegations of authority, continuity locations, vital records, financial/budget guidance, IT continuity (CIT), and strong academic and research continuity. The two clear gaps are devolution (succession is not devolution) and a structured TT&E program (tests/training/exercises are only named in the definition and an after-action step, with no training plan, exercise types, or cadence).

rto_tiers: RTO priority ratings with maximum allowed recovery times: < 4 hours, < 24 hours, < 1 week; each department assigns an RTO to every essential function in its continuity plan
vital_records_approach: Business-unit continuity plan template includes a Vital Records table (name, vendor, primary location, alternate location) plus Publications/Programs-and-Software tables with primary/alternate locations
tte_cadence: Multi-Year Training and Exercise Plan (MYTEP, Appendix E) integrating EM and continuity programs; training in workshops/seminars/role-specific formats and exercises of ranging size/type; after-action reports and a Corrective Action Program; business units exercise plans at least annually
reconstitution_treatment: Framed as the recurring objective of 'return to normal university operations'; Continuity Plan defined as procedures to 'respond, recover, resume, and restore university essential functions to a predefined level'; no distinct reconstitution process/section
academic_continuity_treatment: Program explicitly encompasses 'academic, business, and information technology (IT) continuity of operations plans'; teaching/instruction and research are named essential functions; academic units (all colleges, Academic Senate, Pollak Library) are designated business units that must hold COOPs
it_continuity_treatment: Separate CSUF Information Technology Disaster Recovery Plan (ITDR) under the Division of IT covers recovery of technology facilities/platforms; the Campus Continuity Coordinator aligns DRPs and continuity plans and ensures RTOs/RPOs are communicated to IT with gap remediation
interdependencies_treatment: BIA determines 'essential functions, respective business units, and interdependencies'; business-unit template has a dedicated Interdependencies section; coordination with other CSU campuses and local/state/federal agencies

unusual_decisions.decision: Continuity authority is delegated from the President to the Chief of Police as Campus Continuity Coordinator, with the Emergency Management & Continuity Coordinator as backup and an EM&C Assistant as tertiary backup
unusual_decisions.quote: The President delegates authority to the Chief of Police to prioritize the development, implementation and maintenance of a Cal State Fullerton Continuity Program to ensure compliance with the CSU System Continuity Program.

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly aligns the program's guiding principles to the university's six Guiding Principles of Social Justice
unusual_decisions.quote: Social Justice: The CP intentionally aligns with CSUF's six Guiding Principles of Social Justice, including: Enhance Equitable Learning; Expand Intentional Outreach; Amplify Hispanic Serving Institution Designation & Radiate an Identity of Inclusion; Building a Professorial-to-Leadership Pipeline; Recognize History and Catalyze Change; and Create an Antiracist Community.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats academic, business, and IT continuity as three coordinated COOP streams feeding one campus CONOPS
unusual_decisions.quote: Each individual essential function listed on page 4 has responsibility over their individual plans that feed into the overall CSUF Continuity Concept of Operations Plan (CONOPS), which is maintained by the OEMC.

analyst_notes: Read all 82 pages. CSUF's 2026 Continuity Program is a polished FCD-element/program-level document with a President-signed delegation of authority (12/11/2025), a continuity CONOPS, a hazard/risk assessment (Table B.1), a BIA with three RTO tiers, an essential-functions/business-unit inventory (34 units), a crisis communication plan, a vital-records and interdependencies business-unit template, a separate IT-DR plan, and a MYTEP with AAR/Corrective Action Program. The two clear gaps are orders of succession for leadership positions (only continuity-coordinator backups are named; the word 'succession' never appears) and devolution.

rto_tiers: Annex C prioritizes functions 1-5 each with an RTO; recovery resource needs scoped at 24h / 72h / 1 week / later.
succession_depth: Two named successors per leadership position (Successor 1 / Successor 2).
vital_records_approach: ITS backs up central databases/files to secure offsite locations; per-department vital records, systems and equipment itemized in Annex C under IT DR Policy 4-OP-H-10.
tte_cadence: Recommended at least annually; tabletop exercises plus annual functional IT-recovery tests verifying restore from backup and RTO/RPO.
reconstitution_treatment: Annex A reconstitution checklist: salvage/inventory, long-term recovery plans, transition from alternate site back to a designated facility, staff debrief.
academic_continuity_treatment: Dedicated Continuity of Research section covering specialized utilities, third-party vendors, hazardous materials, and the care and management of research animals; educational priorities referenced.
it_continuity_treatment: Defers to FSU Policy 4-OP-H-10 (IT Disaster Recovery and Backup Data); requires units to maintain a written BC plan for critical assets with recurring backup and recovery procedures.
interdependencies_treatment: Personnel Contact List includes vendors & contractors; resource requirements table includes third-party services and supply chain.

unusual_decisions.decision: Invokes a Florida public-records statute to control distribution of the COOP as sensitive (not classified) information.
unusual_decisions.quote: While a Continuity of Operations Plan (COOP) is not considered a classified document, it does contain sensitive information and its handling and distribution should be controlled and limited, both electronically and in hard copy.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats continuity of research — including live research animals — as a first-class essential function.
unusual_decisions.quote: Applicable research (including the care and management of animals used for education and research), required support resources, and any unique space requirements were considered in the development of this COOP

unusual_decisions.decision: Fixes a 12-hour activation window and a 30-day sustainment horizon as planning parameters.
unusual_decisions.quote: The COOP should be implemented no later than 12 hours after an incident has occurred that has disrupted business operations and provide guidance to sustain operations for up to 30 days.

analyst_notes: Read in full (PDF text, ~35 pp). FSU's university-issued unit COOP template built on the FCD continuity elements (succession, delegations, alert/notification, vital records, research, alternate facilities, communications, essential functions) and mandated by Policy 4-OP-E-10. Operates as a unit-level plan that works in concert with FSU's Primary COOP, so coop_treatment is 'separate'. Strong on the analytic/operational spine (risk table, RTOs, alternate facilities, reconstitution checklist) but, as a template, budget/acquisition and devolution are not addressed.

essential_functions: Essential/Critical Functions per Federal Preparedness Circular 65 (Cal Maritime uses 'Critical Function' for Essential Function): provide vital/mission-critical services, exercise civil authority, maintain public safety, sustain industrial/economic base; mission-critical functions named include instruction to enrolled students, facility security and employee communication; Essential/Critical Function Summary and Detail worksheets
rto_tiers: Four-level criticality classification: Critical 1 = cannot pause, necessary to life/health/security; Critical 2 = continue if at all possible, reduced/alternative mode; Critical 3 = may pause but must resume within 30 days or sooner; Critical 4 = deferrable, resume when conditions permit (applied to both functions and IT systems)
succession_depth: Leadership successor captured for the unit head ('a person who would be an appropriate substitute if the head of the unit is absent'; explicitly noted as usually not an officially-designated position); Key Personnel and Alternates collected
vital_records_approach: 'Vital Records and Information' is a required field in the Essential/Critical Function Detail worksheet (resource requirements), alongside network requirements and facilities/equipment; planning prompts include 'What if network data is not available?'
tte_cadence: Per EO 1014: testing of some parts of the BCP annually and all parts every seven years to identify plan deficiencies; initial training to all individuals responsible for developing/implementing the plan, with additional training as necessary; a review log maintained
reconstitution_treatment: Stated as a goal ('respond to an event and return to performing its essential functions as quickly as possible') but no reconstitution/return-to-normal process; broader recovery referenced out to the Emergency Preparedness Guidelines
academic_continuity_treatment: Instruction to enrolled students named as a mission-critical function; classroom instruction classified Critical 3; work-from-home and remote connectivity action items for faculty/students when office/lab space is damaged; research addressed
it_continuity_treatment: IT system criticality is assessed per department (same four-tier scale), but IT disaster recovery is explicitly out of scope ('The disaster recovery plans for the University’s network and servers are outside the scope of this document')
interdependencies_treatment: Upstream and downstream dependencies captured in the Function Detail worksheet; framing questions 'who produces what we need?' and 'who needs what we produce?'; network requirements (campus network for e-mail/Internet) listed as a resource requirement

unusual_decisions.decision: Anchors essential-function definitions in Federal Preparedness Circular 65 while renaming them 'Critical Functions' for local use
unusual_decisions.quote: An Essential Function (as defined in the Federal Preparedness Circular 65) is a function that enables an organization to [Note: Cal Maritime uses the term Critical Function to describe Essential Functions]

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly carves IT network/server disaster recovery out of the departmental BCP scope
unusual_decisions.quote: The disaster recovery plans for the University’s network and servers are outside the scope of this document.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines delegation of authority as a written instrument and prompts departments to treat action items as ideas rather than resource-bound commitments
unusual_decisions.quote: A formal delegation of authority is an assignment of authority and responsibility to perform specified acts on behalf of the organization. This assignment is almost always granted via a written document.

analyst_notes: A 14-page Department Business Continuity Plan template (v2.0) under CSU EO 1014, which uniquely cites Federal Preparedness Circular 65 for essential functions. It is well-structured around essential/critical functions with a four-tier criticality scale doubling as RTO bands, an effectively BIA-style function-detail worksheet (upstream/downstream dependencies, vital records, network/facility resources and 'what-if' impact prompts), leadership successor and key-personnel capture, a defined delegation of authority, telework/off-campus continuity, plan maintenance with a review log, and EO 1014 testing/training cadence. It is promulgated via versioned approval. It lacks a continuity budget, a systematic threat/hazard assessment, devolution, a reconstitution process, and IT disaster recovery (explicitly out of scope).

essential_functions: Organizational-level functions that must be continued throughout, or resumed as rapidly as possible after, a disruption; units determine priority of and perform unit essential functions in order of priority; Academic Continuity, Administrative Continuity and Research Continuity defined as preservation of academic core, administrative core and research capabilities
succession_depth: Unit order of succession required as a COOP goal; University President succession governed by a separate Delegation of Authority University Operating Procedure (referenced, not enumerated in the policy)
vital_records_approach: COOP goal to 'Establish means for identifying, backing-up and accessing unit vital records, files and databases'; Working Group includes Associate CIO, Information Security Officer and Director of IT Systems Architecture
tte_cadence: COOP Working Group develops and conducts Continuity of Operations training, exercises and continuous-improvement efforts; Emergency Operations Group Planning Team plans training/exercises and maintains the Integrated Preparedness Plan; emergency management teams train and conduct drills with Vermont Dept. of Public Safety partners
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery named as an emergency-management phase and Disaster Recovery defined ('quickly resume mission-essential functions'); Research Continuity references quickly reconstituting research resources; no dedicated institution-level reconstitution/return-to-normal process
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Continuity defined ('preserving the functions of the academic core...including teaching and learning, scholarship, and research'); Registrar assigned Academic Continuity role; Research Continuity separately defined and addressed
it_continuity_treatment: Cyber Security Incident Response Team led by Information Security Officer; ISO 'ensures that a University system-wide, disaster recovery and incident response plan is in place'; IT/Security provides Cybersecurity/IT Support; Telecom and Network Services provide Communications/IT Support
interdependencies_treatment: IT, Procurement, Telecommunications and other support roles enumerated, but no explicit internal/external dependency, vendor, utility or mutual-aid mapping

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines three parallel continuity domains (academic, administrative, research) as first-class concepts in the policy
unusual_decisions.quote: Research Continuity: The capability to maintain critical research equipment, supplies, computers, unique data and specimens/samples, and records during disruptive events, or the capability to quickly reconstitute these resources and resume research after a disruptive event.

unusual_decisions.decision: Charters a standing, IT-heavy Continuity of Operations Working Group to produce the COOP, a simplified BIA and an all-hazard risk heat map
unusual_decisions.quote: produce a simplified business impact analysis; 3) produce and maintain a Continuity of Operations plan; 4) assist department Emergency Response Teams to develop unit continuity of operations plans; and 5) develop and conduct Continuity of Operations training, exercises and continuous improvement efforts.

unusual_decisions.decision: Acknowledges municipal/state authority can supersede University command and employ University assets in a larger response
unusual_decisions.quote: their emergency management functions may, to the extent of applicable law, supersede those of the University and employ University assets in the larger, coordinated response.

analyst_notes: An institutional Emergency Management and Continuity of Operations Policy (v3.25.1, approved by the President, with revision history) that integrates emergency management and COOP under NIMS/ICS and an all-hazards approach. It cleanly defines essential functions and three continuity domains (academic, administrative, research), charters a COOP Working Group to produce a simplified BIA and risk heat map, and lists COOP goals covering succession, delegation, alternate facilities, vital records and human capital. As a policy it stops short of numeric RTOs, dependency mapping, a budget/acquisition program, devolution, and a dedicated reconstitution process. Strong governance and definitional spine; operational detail is deferred to the COOP plan and unit plans it mandates.

essential_functions: Public Safety (emergency response, dispatch, EOC activation)
rto_tiers: Per-process 'Allowable Downtime' (e.g., None/0-1 day/½ day/1-2 days/2-3 days/5 days/peak vs off-peak) plus 'Priority for Recovery' High/Medium/Low across all divisions
vital_records_approach: Unit plans must 'provide for the ongoing back up of critical data and protection of critical equipment' and 'store at least one current copy in an off-site facility with immediate availability'; DIT runs a separate Disaster Recovery Plan for core systems
tte_cadence: Unit BCPs must be exercised at least once every two years (objectives, validation, documented results, corrections); annual unit-head review; training for staff and within 120 days for new employees
reconstitution_treatment: Unit plans must 'establish procedures for return to full, normal operations of the operating unit, including that of non-critical functions'
academic_continuity_treatment: 'Learning and Teaching' is one of three critical-process categories (lecture & teaching, research, continuing education, graduation, student support services); college/library/academic-unit recovery deferred to college-level plans
it_continuity_treatment: Division of Information Technology 'maintains a separate Disaster Recovery Plan that addresses the recovery and the continuity of those services'; DIT core services treated as basic to recovery of all other processes
interdependencies_treatment: BIA explicitly built on 'interdependencies between these processes'; unit plans must ensure depended-on departments and external suppliers also have continuity plans; risk analysis extracted from the campus Disaster Recovery Plan

unusual_decisions.decision: The university-wide document plus all collected unit plans together constitute the complete BCP, with the VP for Student Affairs as compiler
unusual_decisions.quote: The Vice President for Student Affairs will be responsible for collecting all unit plans and the combination of this document and the unit plans will constitute the University's complete business Continuity Plan.

unusual_decisions.decision: Cross-unit prioritization during activation is reserved to the President's Cabinet after the fact rather than pre-scripted
unusual_decisions.quote: In the event plan activation requires prioritization among units for the recovery of services or allocation of limited resources, that prioritization will be accomplished by the President's Cabinet after consideration of the exact circumstances surrounding the plan activation.

unusual_decisions.decision: Activation authority is tiered: unit managers for local events, Cabinet for university-wide events
unusual_decisions.quote: The determination that an interruption has occurred may be made by the individual unit manager for local occurrences or by the Cabinet for university-wide occurrences.

analyst_notes: Read all 14 pages. BSU's BCP is a university-wide, division-by-division plan: a detailed BIA inventory across every division with allowable-downtime and recovery priority, a risk assessment and hazard matrix, a continuity-management process figure (pre-incident planning / incident response / maintenance), a 'Policy' section assigning continuity authority to division EPP committees and the President's Cabinet, and unit-plan requirements covering alternate sites, off-site data backup, activation triggers, and return-to-normal procedures. IT-DR is delegated to a standalone DIT plan. Missing: recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, orders of succession, delegations of authority, resilient/interoperable continuity communications, and devolution.

essential_functions: Critical functions defined per unit via BIA (no enumerated university list); examples reference Education and Human Ecology, Office of the CIO, Optometry, Student Life; functional domains include teaching, learning, research, outreach and engagement, student life, patient care, and supporting business operations
rto_tiers: Units provide RTOs and RPOs annually for the systems/applications they own; BIA gives guidelines on tolerable down time; no fixed tier scheme in the policy
tte_cadence: Units conduct BCP testing exercises every two years, type chosen by the BCM Office and unit (live/simulation, real-world, review/discussion, scenario/tabletop); annual BCP review/approval; BIA every two years; risk assessment every three years; performance scorecard, continuous improvement, lessons learned
reconstitution_treatment: BCRs develop procedures for the 'recovery phases' and the program aims to 'restore the university community back to normal in the case of a disruption'
academic_continuity_treatment: Policy commits to 'ensuring the continuity of teaching, learning, research, outreach and engagement, student life experiences, patient care experiences, business operations, and the critical functions that support them'; academic college clusters represented on the steering committee
it_continuity_treatment: BCR continuity strategies for loss of applications/systems (alternative storage, back-up of systems, manual workaround); Enterprise Security Disaster Recovery sits on the BCM Steering Committee; external references to ISO 27031 IT-DR alignment with ISO 22301
interdependencies_treatment: BCM Office provides an annual 'gap report of operational need versus systems and applications (dependencies) capability'; BCR strategies address supplier/supply-chain loss; RTOs/RPOs communicated across units

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes BCM in a dedicated Business Continuity Management Office in Business and Finance with a cross-cluster Steering Committee reporting to the Risk Management Committee and Board of Trustees
unusual_decisions.quote: The Business Continuity Management Office in the Office of Business and Finance is responsible for directing business continuity management (BCM) for the university and units.

unusual_decisions.decision: Aligns the program explicitly with recognized BCM standards and external bodies
unusual_decisions.quote: To set requirements for BCM, maintain continued availability of critical functions, and align university practices with BCM industry standards.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats noncompliance as an enforceable policy matter with tracked exceptions and possible corrective action up to termination
unusual_decisions.quote: The university may enforce corrective action, up to and including termination, in accordance with applicable policies or rules.

analyst_notes: Read all 5 pages. Ohio State's Business Continuity Management University Policy (issued 2020, edited 2023 and 2025) is a strong governance framework: a promulgated university policy with a responsible office, explicit alignment to ISO 22301/ISO 27031 and BCI/DRII, critical-function/BIA/risk-assessment requirements, RTO/RPO collection, a BCM Office and Steering Committee, biennial multi-type testing exercises with a performance scorecard and continuous improvement, alternate-site and IT-recovery strategies, and academic/patient-care continuity. As a policy it does not establish orders of succession, formal delegations of authority, a resilient continuity-communications capability, a vital-records program, activation triggers/decision authority, or devolution.

essential_functions: (College-wide reference list provided: College Leadership; Safety and Security; Communications; Basic Services; Information Technology)
rto_tiers: Template frames essential functions as those with a Recovery Time Objective of 0-24 hours, with instruction to note functions whose RTO is greater than 24 hours; Critical Interruption Worksheet records per-function tolerable downtime (e.g., claims function 'could be delayed by a few days at most').
succession_depth: 'Line of Succession' with Primary/Secondary/Tertiary slots (worked example fills Primary = Ken Lyons, Risk Manager; Secondary/Tertiary N/A).
vital_records_approach: No dedicated vital-records register; critical IT systems/databases listed by priority with hosting location, and workaround processes specified if technology unavailable.
tte_cadence: Captures 'Date of last testing of your restoration process' and 'Date of next testing'; worked example cites the COVID-19 remote-work period (Mar 23 - May 1, 2020) as the last test; next testing 'TBD'.
reconstitution_treatment: 'Resumption of Normal Operations' section: 'Once authorities have deemed it safe to return to the office, key personnel would simply pick up where they left off working remotely.'
it_continuity_treatment: Technology Equipment and critical IT systems/software/databases tables (priority, hosting on/off campus) plus an explicit workaround narrative ('operations can still function through the use of mobile hotspots, smart phones, tablets, etc.').
interdependencies_treatment: Separate Internal Dependencies (e.g., e-mail, phone, shared drive, OneDrive provided by IT, frequency Continuous) and External Dependencies (consultation, claims processing vendors with frequency) tables.

unusual_decisions.decision: Uses a real prior event (COVID-19 remote work) as evidence the restoration process was tested
unusual_decisions.quote: Accountability, Risk and Compliance worked remotely due to the COVID-19 pandemic for a period of 6weeks in early 2020. The department was able to effectively resume its functions with no downtime.

unusual_decisions.decision: Embeds a pre-work 'Critical Interruption Worksheet' that asks how long the unit can function without power, comms, and enterprise systems (e.g., Banner)
unusual_decisions.quote: How long can your department be without the use of enterprise information systems (ex:Banner)?Indefinitely. Insurance claims reporting and management do not require the use of Banner or any other similar system.

analyst_notes: A departmental COOP template completed as a worked example for STC's Accountability, Risk & Compliance unit; explicitly essential-functions-based and tied to activation of the STC All Hazards Emergency Plan. Strong coverage of essential functions, RTO framing, succession, internal/external dependencies, alternate (telework) site, IT workarounds, restoration testing dates, and resumption of normal operations. Legitimately omits a formal BIA, threat/hazard assessment, standards citations, budgeting, delegations of authority, and devolution.

essential_functions: plus a tiered hierarchy of State Essential Functions, Institutional Essential Functions, Primary Mission Essential Functions, Mission Essential Functions, Essential Supporting Activities, and Mission Enhancing Activities defined per College/Division/School/Office
rto_tiers: Defines Max Tolerable Downtime (MTD), Recovery Time Objective (RTO), and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) for each MEF; PMEFs must remain continuous or resume within 12 hours and be maintained up to 30 days or until normal operations resume; BIA scores impact 0 (none) to 5 (severe)
vital_records_approach: BPA Step 9 requires identifying 'standard operating procedures, essential records, and reference materials'; MEF data sheet documents required records; no central vital-records protection/storage program (responsibility pushed to departmental plans)
tte_cadence: Continuity Coordinators attend initial and annual continuity training; Deans/VPs ensure plans are 'developed, coordinated, exercised, and capable of being implemented'; emergency action procedures activated during continuity exercises; BIA review/validation at least every two years; no exercise-type structure or formal after-action/improvement cycle
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct process; 'until normal operations resume' is a stated endpoint only
academic_continuity_treatment: Teaching & Learning (UEF 5), Student Opportunity & Success (UEF 4), and Research (UEF 2) are University Essential Functions; framework relies on remote work, noting COVID-19 showed many MEFs can continue virtually while some have mandatory in-person components
it_continuity_treatment: BPA Step 7 requires identifying communications/information-system requirements, data-access needs, system dependencies and interfaces; RPO defined; but no IT disaster-recovery program, backup strategy, or alternate-processing capability is established (identification only)
interdependencies_treatment: Strong: work-function inventory links interdependencies, BPA Step 4 identifies dependencies/interdependencies (partners, points of contact, support type, timelines), and MEFs are prioritized when they support multiple PMEFs across Colleges/Divisions

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds a seven-level essential-functions hierarchy (State -> Institutional -> University -> Primary Mission -> Mission -> Essential Supporting Activities -> Mission Enhancing Activities) cascading from State of Maryland and USM requirements down to office level
unusual_decisions.quote: Essential functions are the building blocks of continuity... Each level us supported by the functions below, and dependent on the functions above.

unusual_decisions.decision: Maps the five University Essential Functions directly onto the five pillars of the UMBC Strategic Plan
unusual_decisions.quote: UMBC has five UEFs that mirror the five strategic pillars detailed in the UMBC Strategic Plan and form the basis for all our continuity decisions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Deliberately designs the framework for non-expert staff for whom continuity is a collateral duty, generating data sheets automatically from online BPA/BIA worksheets
unusual_decisions.quote: simplified planning and training will help minimize confusion and enable effective decision-making in a time of crisis.

analyst_notes: Read all 30 pages. NOTE: the supplied 2025-UMB-COOP-Plan.pdf URL was a different institution (University of Maryland, Baltimore) and returned HTTP 404; this object codes the document actually mapped to the umbc.edu police-site URL given, UMBC's EOP Annex 3 COOP (Rev. May 2026), a framework/methodology plan. It is a strategic-level framework with a signed approval (Chief of Police), USM VI-13.00 / State of Maryland MDEM COOP-guide authority, an integrated-response concept of operations, a rigorous four-phase planning process (Identify MEFs -> BPA -> BIA -> Prioritize/Document), MTD/RTO/RPO, an HVRA-based threat assessment, deep interdependency mapping, budget/resource and essential-records identification, continuity personnel (CWG/EMAG), activation triggers, and academic continuity via remote work. Because it is a planning framework that pushes execution to departmental DEAPs, it omits orders of succession, formal delegations of authority, a resilient continuity-communications capability, devolution, a reconstitution process, and an IT disaster-recovery program (IT needs are identified per-MEF but no DR/backup program is established).

essential_functions: Critical Operational Services (college-wide infrastructure disaster response/recovery)
Essential Operational Services (protect assets / support educational activities)

Areas of Critical Responsibility: Admissions & Records, Athletics, Auxiliary Services, Business/Finance, Career & Technical, Community Relations, eLearning, EH&S, Facilities, Health Science, HR, IT, Instructional Services, Campus Police, Residential Life, Student Affairs
rto_tiers: Four-tier targeted RTO / minimum RRO table: Critical Support = maintained at all times; Critical Operational = 1 day (RRO 3 days); Essential Operational = 3 days (RRO 3 weeks); Important = 3 weeks (RRO ~1 year)
succession_depth: Incident Commander succession by campus, ranked 'highest available person' (Fulton 3 deep, Tupelo 2 deep, Belden 2 deep); no broader org orders of succession
vital_records_approach: Not addressed as vital records; data center repair/IT recovery site covers data restoration but no vital-records identification/protection program
tte_cadence: Quarterly ERT meetings; >=1 EMC meeting/year; one tabletop exercise/year; building-evacuation drills/testing; annual ERT plan exercises; after-action Program Review
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery of Normal College Operations and gradual return to normal; College-wide Recovery steps executed if impact exceeds 72 hours; Crisis and Continuity Program Review revises documentation
academic_continuity_treatment: eLearning pivot: 'By utilizing eLearning and the Internet, maintaining ongoing academics should be possible under emergency situations even where multiple buildings are damaged, destroyed or if access to the campus is denied'; Instructional Services prepared to use eLearning; backup EOC at eLearning Center
it_continuity_treatment: IT maintains/restores intranet, data, video, voice, wireless; activates IT recovery site if severe; comprehensive damage assessment and repair/replacement of main data center; alternative emergency communications if cell towers down; appendix glossary covers redundant/hot/warm/cold site concepts
interdependencies_treatment: Mutual aid agreements with all state-supported higher-ed institutions and local high schools (athletics); outsourced food/water/bookstore providers required to have Business Continuity Plans; Appendix E Critical Suppliers and Contractors

unusual_decisions.decision: Frames recovery time tiers in business-survival terms tied to semester cancellation and institutional viability
unusual_decisions.quote: On the other hand, if this cannot be accomplished within three weeks or so, the College may be facing the cancellation of a semester and a refunding of tuitions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires all outsourced service providers to maintain their own continuity plans as a contracting condition
unusual_decisions.quote: Outsourcing – Food, water and bookstore operations are outsourced on an ongoing basis. All outsource providers are required to have “Business Continuity Plans” in place.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates physical bunkering / pre-positioning mitigation (storm shutters, sandbags, sump pumps) ahead of forecastable hazards
unusual_decisions.quote: Bunkering – The College has storm shutters for most critical buildings that can be deployed in advance of a tornado. The College also has sandbags and sump pumps that can be deployed in advance of a flood.

analyst_notes: A 67-page institutional COOP integrated with a Crisis/Risk Management Plan and departmental Emergency Operations Plans, organized around six emergency-response priorities and a four-action-step recovery flow (Immediate Actions, Initial Recovery, College-wide Recovery, Program Review). Strongest on RTO/RRO tiering, recovery strategies, alternate/recovery sites, mutual aid and TT&E. Notably lacks formal FCD/NIMS doctrine citation, a true BIA, a hazard/vulnerability assessment, delegations of authority, vital-records management and devolution. Succession exists only for the Incident Commander role.

essential_functions: (Service/asset based) Mission-critical IT services and applications; Data Center operations; network/LAN/core/wireless; telecommunications (VOIP); business applications and databases identified by the Infrastructure Emergency Response Team (IERT)
rto_tiers: Explicit Recovery Response Times: Data Recovery 30 minutes to an hour (volume-dependent); System Recovery - Simple Systems < 1 day, Moderately Complex Systems > 1 day < 3 days, Complex Systems > 3 days. Security-standards section further requires departmental BCPs to document Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO).
vital_records_approach: Recovery teams must ensure documentation for standards, operations, and 'vital records maintenance' and application programs is stored securely and reassembled at standby facilities; data backups classified, archived offsite (CCCD IT Data Center), retained 1 year on media, 15-day on-site tape retention, disk-to-disk-to-tape with offsite vaulting.
tte_cadence: The IERT 'is responsible for testing of the disaster recovery plan at least annually to ensure the viability of the plan.'
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery teams (Operations, Network) restore computer processing and data/wide-area communications to minimum processing requirements and reassemble at standby facilities; framed as system restoration/recovery rather than a separate reconstitution phase.
it_continuity_treatment: This is the document's core: enterprise backup solution (daily/weekly/monthly), offsite tape vaulting, server/workstation rebuild-from-template strategy, dual Data Centers with partial CRAC/UPS/generator redundancy, network and VOIP redundancy posture, recovery response times, and recovery-team roles - backed by a full Information Technology Security Standards manual.
interdependencies_treatment: Identifies vendor dependencies for recovery (e.g., Avaya and Carousel for VOIP) and assigns Networks/Operations teams to restore inter-system links; departmental BCPs assign Business Recovery Coordinators who coordinate across business areas.

unusual_decisions.decision: Adopts a rebuild-from-template recovery model and deliberately does not back up workstations or static data
unusual_decisions.quote: Workstations are not backed up as: all business data is stored on servers and workstations, like servers, can be recreated from build templates.

unusual_decisions.decision: Separates departmental Business Continuity Plans (with Business Recovery Coordinators) from the IT Disaster Recovery Plan, defining the relationship between them
unusual_decisions.quote: Each business area is responsible for documenting a Business Continuity Plan and designating a Business Recovery Coordinator who will develop and maintain their plan and participate in notification and recovery activities.

analyst_notes: An IT Disaster Recovery Plan for Orange Coast College (Coast Community College District) bundled with a comprehensive Information Technology Security Standards and Protocols manual designed to meet PCI-DSS. The DR plan is strong on backups/offsite storage, data-center and network redundancy, explicit recovery-response/recovery-time tiers, recovery-team roles, vital-records-maintenance-at-standby-facilities, annual DR testing, and it points to departmental BCPs that must carry RTO/RPO and disaster-declaration/activation procedures. Standards alignment is present (PCI-DSS). As an IT-DR document it legitimately lacks academic-instructional continuity, a formal BIA, an enterprise threat/hazard assessment, leadership succession/delegations, and devolution.

essential_functions: Functions deemed critical to the continuity of UM business operations (evaluated by the Policy Group)
Departmental Business Continuity Plans identify critical functions and staff to maintain essential functions during emergency operations
Continuation of academic activities (Academic Impact Unit) and restoration of essential services
succession_depth: University President Succession of Authority (line of succession if President unavailable); Liaison Officer Succession of Authority; alternate staff designations for unavailable EOC positions
tte_cadence: Dedicated Training and Exercises section; EHS coordinates after-action reviews; EOP updated as informed by drills and exercises; CPRG reviews EOP annually
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery section plus Deactivation, Demobilization & Plan Reassessment; Business and Administrative Continuity restores administrative/business functions; Resumption of Academic Learning with Policy Group initial recovery planning
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Impact Unit addresses academic impacts with the Business Continuity Branch; Resumption of Academic Learning: 'This may involve relocating students, conducting classes in alternative locations, implementing online learning and implementing temporary academic and financial procedures'
it_continuity_treatment: Not addressed as IT disaster recovery (CIO is on the EOP distribution list, but no system contingency/backup/alternate-processing content)
interdependencies_treatment: Heavy reliance on Missoula City and County for law enforcement/fire resources and statewide/regional response; Liaison Officer and Joint Information Center interface with external agencies; control of large incidents passes by statute to responding external agency

unusual_decisions.decision: Scales the University's role inversely to incident magnitude, ceding command to external agencies as scope grows
unusual_decisions.quote: The larger the emergency in terms of area affected, people at risk and magnitude of external response, the smaller relative role UM plays in the overall response.

unusual_decisions.decision: Stands up a dedicated Business Continuity Branch within the EOC Operations Section and a separate Academic Impact Unit
unusual_decisions.quote: The Academic Impact Unit will address needs, concerns and issues related to academic impacts resulting from the emergency in concert with the Business Continuity Branch under the Operations Section.

unusual_decisions.decision: Tasks the Policy Group to formally prepare a delegation of authority when mobilizing the incident management team
unusual_decisions.quote: Mobilize the incident mgt. team, prepare a delegation of authority, and be available to liaise with the Team on University priorities.

analyst_notes: A 2023-24 all-hazards Emergency Operations Plan built on NIMS/ICS with ESF-style functional annexes, signed via a Letter of Promulgation (President, AVP Campus Operations, EHS Director). Although primarily a response plan, it embeds meaningful continuity content: a Business Continuity Branch and Academic Impact Unit, President and Liaison Officer succession of authority, an incident delegation of authority, departmental BCPs identifying essential functions, a recovery section with deactivation/demobilization/reassessment, and resumption of academic learning (online/alternative locations). It lacks a continuity budget, BIA, RTOs, dedicated alternate continuity facilities, vital-records management, devolution and IT disaster recovery, reflecting its EOP (rather than COOP) center of gravity.

rto_tiers: Allowable-downtime tiers per critical mission function: 0-24h / 0-48h / 24-48h / 24-72h / 48+h, plus priority High/Medium/Low; per-function MAD and RTO in worksheets (e.g., Finance MAD 40 hours, RTO 24-40 hours; Academic Affairs RTO 24-72 hours)
vital_records_approach: Databases backed up nightly/weekly with copies stored off-site (bank vault safe-deposit box holds DRP, backup media, administrator info); manual checks held in Administrative Services vault; functional backup rather than a labeled vital-records program
tte_cadence: States BCP 'is exercised and reviewed annually'; some work-around procedures marked tested; no structured TT&E program, exercise types, training plan, or after-action/improvement cycle described
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct return-to-normal process; recovery framed per-function
academic_continuity_treatment: Strong: Academic Affairs worksheet pivots instruction to Blackboard Ultra online platform (all courses have master online shells, faculty trained), relocation to nearest campus or external/local-school sites; MAD 24-72h tied to TCSG instructional contact-hour standards
it_continuity_treatment: Detailed IT Disaster Recovery Plan covering 13+ systems (telephone, network, PeopleSoft, Banner, Active Directory, email, web, document imaging, etc.) with pre-disaster protection (nightly/weekly off-site backups, bank-vault storage), post-disaster recovery steps, RTO priorities and cost/time estimates
interdependencies_treatment: Per-function vendor/utility/contractor dependencies identified (Parker Fiber, AT&T/BellSouth, GTA, Georgia Power, banks), reliance on TCSG and other technical colleges for mutual aid and alternate sites

unusual_decisions.decision: Plan is built entirely as per-division fill-in worksheets submitted annually to the state system office rather than a single narrative COOP
unusual_decisions.quote: Once the Committees' review is complete the Plan is reviewed by the College President and submitted to the Emergency Manager at TCSG by May 1 st of each year.

unusual_decisions.decision: Cashiering/finance continuity drops to hand-written receipts reconciled in Excel until Banner is restored
unusual_decisions.quote: If Banner is unavailable, a hand-written receipt will be given to the student and the receipt number will be recorded on the excel spreadsheet. Student detail information will be recorded and reconciled every day manually in an excel spreadsheet until the system is restored.

unusual_decisions.decision: Operating bank account kept accessible to three signatories so any one can act if the others are unavailable
unusual_decisions.quote: We would have to make sure that our operating account remained fully accessible to at least three different people with security to make decisions in the absence of one or both of the other two.

analyst_notes: Read all 30 pages. ATC's 2025-2026 BCP is a state-mandated (TCSG Policy 3.4.1) departmental-worksheet plan: signed promulgation page, Critical Mission Functions chart with allowable-downtime tiers, a Hazard Vulnerability Assessment, per-division recovery worksheets (Finance, Economic Development, IT, Student Affairs, Academic Affairs), an embedded IT-DRP, and emergency contacts. Strongest on academic continuity (online pivot) and IT-DR; absent are succession orders, delegations of authority, a four-phase CONOPS, devolution, reconstitution-as-process, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, and a structured TT&E program.

essential_functions: Level 1: Computer Services Area & Central Computer Room
Level 2: Central Telephone Services; 911 Emergency Services; Network Infrastructure and Services; Cable Plant

Level 3: File & Print Services; ERP Services (CAMS); Email Services; Web Services; Campus Card Services; Student Residential Network (ResNet); Academic Instructional Technology Classrooms; Student Computer Laboratory Services
rto_tiers: Three severity/priority levels driving recovery order: Level 1 (central computer room/servers - highest priority), Level 2 (network and VOIP/911), Level 3 (specific applications/services); 'Level 1 risks will be given increased priority over other levels...same holds true for Level 2 versus Level 3 risks.' (priority-tier recovery rather than numeric RTO/RPO)
vital_records_approach: Backup/Recovery Tapes defined as copies of all software and data on central servers used to return servers to pre-incident readiness; email retention governed by the GC Information Handling Backup and Retention Standard; no formal vital-records register.
reconstitution_treatment: General Disaster Response and Recovery Guidelines plus per-service restore procedures (isolate cause, restore service, assess workarounds incl. temporary outsourcing, notify campus when restored); framed as service restoration, not a separate reconstitution phase.
academic_continuity_treatment: Addressed only through IT-service recovery for instruction: Level 3 'Academic Instructional Technology Classrooms' (Technology Enhanced Classrooms and dual-purpose student computer labs) and Student Computer Laboratory Services; no academic-calendar or instructional-delivery continuity.
it_continuity_treatment: The document's core: a phased IT Disaster Recovery Plan with introduction, objectives, assumptions, disaster definition, general response/recovery guidelines, a three-level IT Risk Assessment covering each major service area, backup/recovery tapes, environmental controls, an alternate restore site (Van Alstyne), maintenance section, and appendices (DR Action Items, Vendor Contacts).

unusual_decisions.decision: Candidly documents single points of failure - no full offsite backup facility and no fire suppression in the computer room
unusual_decisions.quote: There is no off-site backup facility, currently identified, that could entirely replace the functions of the Central Computer Room if it is rendered inoperable by an environmental or manmade disaster. The Van Alstyne facility however could be used to restore our on-line presence

unusual_decisions.decision: Subordinates all IT recovery priorities to life safety in its planning assumptions
unusual_decisions.quote: The safety of students, staff, and faculty is of paramount; the safeguard of such will supersede concerns specific to hardware, software, and other recovery needs.

analyst_notes: Grayson College's 2016-2020 Technology Plan with an embedded IT Disaster Recovery Plan (revision history from 2009, last updated 12-11-2020). The DR plan is phase/level-based: it uses a three-level 'IT Risk Assessment' that doubles as a priority-tier recovery scheme, defines disaster declaration/activation, backup-and-recovery tapes, environmental risks, an alternate restore facility (Van Alstyne), and a maintenance section with a document change page (version control). Distinctively honest about single points of failure. As an IT-DR plan it lacks a formal BIA, enterprise interdependency mapping, leadership succession/delegations, vital-records register, devolution, continuity-standards citations, and budget detail; academic continuity appears only as classroom/lab IT-service recovery.

essential_functions: Curriculum Instruction (provide instruction for all programs; maintain Programs of Study; faculty contracts)
Student Services: Veterans (VA-ONCE certification), Financial Aid, Registration & Admissions, Counseling
Administration: Payroll & Fringe, Cash Receipting, Accounts Receivable, Purchasing, Accounts Payable, Financial Reporting
rto_tiers: Per-function 'Maximum amount time area could operate without function' (MTD-style): ranges from None (instruction, faculty contracts) to 1 hour or less (financial aid/registration), 1 day (cash receipting), 2 days (library collection/circulation), 30 days (veterans). No numeric RTO/RPO bands.
succession_depth: Not addressed (cross-training and 'backup' staff named per function, but no orders of succession)
vital_records_approach: IT backup procedures: daily full backups (Datatel via UFSdump on DLT tape autoloader; AD/Netware via Veritas); backup logs reviewed; tapes stored in a 2nd on-campus location (Grier Science Building) and removed by staff if computer room threatened; cloud-based survivability for Moodle/Gmail; off-campus system backups for payroll/AR
tte_cadence: No continuity-plan TT&E program; general institutional emergency procedures 'exercised...no less often than once a year' via safety committee
reconstitution_treatment: Explicit 'Return to Normal Operations (Reconstitution Phase)' in IT Continuity Plan and Disaster Recovery Plan: recovery activities terminated and normal operations restored/transferred back to MCC's computer operations facility
academic_continuity_treatment: Distance/online learning continuity: back-up Blackboard server or alternate distance-learning delivery; Moodle cloud-based; temporary instructional locations via use agreements with local school systems; cross-training to backup non-instructional functions
it_continuity_treatment: Full embedded IT Contingency Plan + Disaster Recovery Plan (pages 18-77): CONOPS, BIA, disaster risks/prevention, information-security policies, backup procedures, disaster notification list, damage assessment, alternate site (any wired building / off-site processing), return-to-normal/reconstitution
interdependencies_treatment: NCCCS System Office (NCCCSO) and mutual aid sites for compatible backups; common course library; payment services; off-site payroll/AR processing; cloud vendors (Gmail/Moodle); use agreements with Iredell/Statesville and Mooresville school systems; local healthcare agency equipment

unusual_decisions.decision: Directs IT staff to physically remove and personally safeguard the latest backup tape if the computer room is threatened, but never at personal risk
unusual_decisions.quote: In the event of a threat to the computer room facilities, administrative computer department personnel shall remove the latest backup tape from the computer room and maintain personal possession until a secure environment can be obtained. In no case shall personnel place themselves in undue personal jeopardy to obtain the tape from the computer room.

unusual_decisions.decision: Reserves use of an alternate site for catastrophic full-campus shutdown to the President's discretion
unusual_decisions.quote: In this event, select applications such as payroll could be performed by use of an alternate site at the discretion of the President.

unusual_decisions.decision: Built the entire plan on a 2004 NCCCS state mandate for a Financial Services Continuity Plan, then broadened it institution-wide
unusual_decisions.quote: The North Carolina Community College System (NCCCS) mandated in 2004 that each college should implement an appropriate Financial Services Continuity Plan to “ensure the timely delivery of critical functions and services to its stakeholders”.

analyst_notes: A 2011-2012 plan in two distinct halves: a department-by-department Risk Assessment / Business Continuity section (critical function, MTD, and facilities/personnel/technology threats-and-plans per unit) and a robust embedded IT Contingency + Disaster Recovery Plan with CONOPS, BIA, backup procedures and an explicit reconstitution phase. Driven by the NCCCS state mandate. Strong on essential functions, BIA, IT-DR, vital-data backup and reconstitution; weak on promulgation authority, formal maintenance cadence, succession, delegations, resilient continuity communications, devolution and a continuity-specific TT&E program. A 'Reviewed May 11, 2007' signature block exists but functions as a review record rather than a promulgation grant.

essential_functions: Department's functions essential to operational continuity and/or recovery, each with responsible position title, primary and alternate (cross-trained) personnel (Section F worksheet); university objectives include continuing functions essential to university operations
succession_depth: Leadership Succession (Section G): Head of Operations, First Successor, Second Successor, Third Successor (3 deep) who can make operational decisions if the head is absent
vital_records_approach: Section E Emergency Access to Information and Systems: identify critical data/records, whether backed up on-site or off-site (off-site data backup, backup files on flash drives, hard copies), and simulate a failure scenario that tests the ability to recover lost critical data
tte_cadence: Section K Exercising Your Plan: staff orientation meeting, call tree drill, tabletop exercise, interdepartmental exercise, emergency assembly drill, emergency communication test, off-site information access test, unscheduled work-at-home day, with scheduled dates and staff distribution date
reconstitution_treatment: Section L Recovery: plan to fully resume operations as soon as possible after the crisis, addressing resumption/scheduling of normal activities, work backlog, resupply of inventories, absenteeism and emotional needs
academic_continuity_treatment: University objective to 'Resume normal teaching, research and service operations as soon as possible'; departments classify principal nature of operations (Instruction, laboratory/other research, research support); departments state teaching/research/service objectives
it_continuity_treatment: Section E covers emergency access to information/systems: remote access (or authorization), contacting IT support, Blackboard, off-site data backup, backup files, hard copies, alternate email; includes a recovery-test of lost critical data
interdependencies_treatment: Section H Key Internal (within NMSU) Dependencies (ICT, Payroll, Purchasing, Business & Finance, Fire/Police, HR, Facilities & Services, plus department-listed) and Section I Key External Dependencies (suppliers/providers with primary/alternate, encouraged to prepare their own COOP)

unusual_decisions.decision: Embeds employee household/family preparedness into the institutional COOP on the premise that staff will not report if their families are unsafe
unusual_decisions.quote: Past experience has taught us that employees may not show up for work if they are concerned for the safety and security of their families.

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds a recoverability self-test directly into the template by asking departments to simulate a data-loss scenario
unusual_decisions.quote: Simulate a failure scenario that tests the ability to recover “lost” critical data.

unusual_decisions.decision: Encourages departments to push continuity planning out to their external suppliers
unusual_decisions.quote: We recommend that you encourage them to prepare continuity of operations plan.

analyst_notes: A six-page fillable All-Hazards COOP template for NMSU departments, structured around essential functions and recovery and submitted to a Dean/VP for approval. Despite its brevity it captures an unusually broad set of components: an all-hazards threat list, essential functions with primary/alternate cross-trained personnel, three-deep leadership succession, internal and external dependency inventories, emergency communications with backups, emergency access to systems (off-site data backup and a recovery self-test), mitigation strategies including telework, an exercise menu with dates, and a recovery section. It lacks named doctrine, a continuity budget, a maintenance cadence, a true BIA, RTOs, formal delegations of authority with triggers, explicit activation procedures and devolution.

essential_functions: IT systems necessary to recover essential business functions or dependent business functions (each identified with RTO/RPO); identification of all critical data, applications, documentation, personnel, facilities and support items necessary during recovery
rto_tiers: Recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs) established by System/Data Owners for each IT system, based on the BIA and Risk Assessment; alternate processing site resumption within an organization-defined time period consistent with RTO/RPO
vital_records_approach: Data Backup and Restoration plan with secure off-site storage of backup media; full/incremental backup scheme; periodic test restores; retention per State Library of Virginia records retention policies; e-discovery readiness
tte_cadence: Backup/recovery procedures tested at least annually (not to exceed one year between tests, documented); annual exercise (or more often) of the VSU COOP and IT DR components; review and revision of IT DR components following each exercise
reconstitution_treatment: Transfer and resumption of information system operations for essential missions/business functions at an alternate processing site; ensures IT services can be resumed following an event causing loss of data
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (IT contingency scope; academic continuity resides in the broader VSU COOP)
it_continuity_treatment: Entire policy is IT contingency planning / disaster recovery: BIA-driven IT-system RTO/RPO, backup and recovery plan, off-site and alternate storage site with agreements, alternate processing site with equivalent security safeguards, test restores, incident notification, local/remote access communication methods
interdependencies_treatment: Recovers 'essential business functions or dependent business functions'; scope covers contractors, employees, vendors and student interns; alternate storage/processing sites require necessary agreements; business partners follow best practices

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates a dedicated IT 'TS COOP Coordinator' to collaborate with the university COOP coordinator as the focal point bridging IT-DR and institutional COOP
unusual_decisions.quote: The Technology Services (TS) Continuity of Operations (COOP) Coordinator is the designated IT employee responsible for collaboration with the university’s Continuity Plan (CP) Coordinator as the focal point for IT aspects of Continuity Plan and related Disaster Recovery (DR) planning activities.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires the alternate storage and processing sites to provide information-security safeguards equivalent to the primary site
unusual_decisions.quote: ensures that the alternate storage site provides information security safeguards equivalent to that of the primary site.
