unusual_decisions.decision: Ties electronic-records management explicitly to legal-discovery producibility and the State Library of Virginia retention schedule
unusual_decisions.quote: Retention of the data handled by an IT system must be in accordance with the State Library of Virginia records retention policies and procedures.

analyst_notes: A five-page IT Contingency Planning Policy (6135, approved by the President 9/6/17) structured around NIST 800-34/800-53-style contingency controls though without naming the doctrine in-text. It is explicitly the IT-DR component of the university's broader VSU COOP, assigning roles to System/Data Owners and a TS COOP Coordinator and requiring a BIA, IT-system RTOs/RPOs, backup/restoration with off-site and alternate storage, an alternate processing site, test restores, annual exercises and post-exercise revision. Promulgated by the President. As an IT-DR policy it omits a CONOPS, succession, delegations, activation triggers, devolution and academic continuity, which live in the parent COOP.

rto_tiers: Per-function RTO column on Worksheet #1: Conducting classes < 1 Week; Maintaining animal living environment 24 Hours; Payroll < 1 Week; Research < 1 Week. Each function also has an Impact and Priority (all Priority 1).
succession_depth: Worksheet #4 'Orders of Succession' provides a line-of-succession table (Name/Title/Alternate Name/Alternate Title) with rule that a higher person resuming availability assumes responsibility; table is a blank/sample fill-in (Director shown), depth not numerically fixed.
vital_records_approach: Worksheet #3 'Vital Records, Forms and Documents' lists each record with storage location, format, update frequency, backup method, and point of contact; recommends copying web-based forms to DVD/CD/USB for emergency use.
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery checklist (Sections H/I) includes long-term recovery steps ('Develop long term recovery plans', 'Test systems to ensure they are working properly', 'Conduct transition of all functions, personnel'), but no distinct return-to-normal/reconstitution phase is labeled.
academic_continuity_treatment: Conducting classes is treated as a critical function (RTO < 1 Week) with alternatives (hold classes off campus, arrange transportation, use local aquariums' facilities/labs); no broader academic-calendar or instructional-continuity program.
it_continuity_treatment: Worksheet #2 maps software applications supporting critical functions with location, backup method and BC/external support contacts; recovery checklist includes contacting IT Help Desk, ordering replacement hardware, testing user logins, and restoring site-specific services.
interdependencies_treatment: Each critical function on Worksheet #1 lists Dependencies (power, key personnel, working computers, lab/equipment); applications worksheet identifies internal (BC Data Center) and external (vendor) support providers.

unusual_decisions.decision: Built-in cross-training matrix pairing each function with primary performers and named cross-trained backups
unusual_decisions.quote: Worksheet #11: Essential Personnel and Cross-TrainingFunction Performs this Function Can be Cross Trained Commentspayroll 1. Dean2. Associate Dean1. Professor

unusual_decisions.decision: Living-animal welfare drives the tightest RTO and a pre-arranged reciprocal aquarium agreement
unusual_decisions.quote: agreements have been made with local aquarium in the event animals have to be moved. The aquarium will provide tanks, food, and vehicles and crates to transport animals

analyst_notes: A fully worked departmental BCP template (illustrated with a fictional 'Deep Sea Studies Department'), organized around FCD-style program elements expressed as worksheets: critical functions + RTO, applications, vital records, resources/personnel, succession, alternate facilities, and a detailed activation/recovery checklist. As a template it legitimately omits a real BIA, formal risk/threat assessment, standards citations, budgeting, delegations of authority, devolution, and a TT&E program. Reconstitution is only implied within the recovery checklist rather than as a distinct phase.

succession_depth: Chief Public Information Officer (CPIO) role shared by the President and named proxies, one per building/area (Ron Layne COO, Dr. Rebecca Roush, Kellie Shoemake, Dr. Fallon Brewington, Germaine Elkins, Dr. Julie Voigt, Dr. Twana Mc...)
vital_records_approach: Not addressed (off-campus backup referenced only as a Campus-Level risk: 'off-campus backup failures')
tte_cadence: Emergency Response Procedures distributed annually via mandatory employee training; per-sector 'Periodic Campus Testing or Training' (e.g., annual network-closet/WiFi inspection; ongoing end-user training; ongoing review with Risk Assessment Team and Emergency Management Team)
devolution_treatment: Not addressed (CPIO proxies are a succession/role-sharing mechanism, not devolution to a separate unit/site)
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct phase; 'recovery' appears within each sector's Intervention and Recovery Strategy but no return-to-normal/reconstitution process
academic_continuity_treatment: Sector 6 E-learning Access Interruptions: faculty-based e-learning team maintains and monitors student access for virtual/hybrid courses; impact-level intervention and recovery for learning platforms
it_continuity_treatment: Sector 1 covers IT/Business interruptions with five impact levels (User/Classroom/Building/Campus/NCCCS-System), points-of-contact, ransomware/phishing emergency response, and the NCCCS Cyber Incident Response Call Tree (NC Joint Cyber Security Task Force, MCNC NOC); no formal DR plan with backups/alternate processing
interdependencies_treatment: NCCCS system-level dependencies (Colleague, IIPS, common course library, payment services); MCNC Network Operations Center; NC Joint Cyber Security Task Force; auxiliary campuses (Hoke County, Robbins Center, Larry Caddell Training Center)

unusual_decisions.decision: Uses a five-tier 'impact zone' escalation (User to NCCCS-system level) instead of time-based recovery objectives to drive intervention and formality
unusual_decisions.quote: The scale of impact determines whether Intervention and Recovery involves additional guidance and /or formal procedures.

unusual_decisions.decision: Distributes the Chief Public Information Officer authority across the President and building-assigned proxies
unusual_decisions.quote: A key figure in implementation of any portion of this plan is the Chief Public Information Officer (CPIO)—a title shared by the college President, Brenda Jackson, and her proxies in that role

unusual_decisions.decision: Embeds a real, dated cyber incident-response example (spoofed VoIP voicemail phishing) directly into the IT sector as operational guidance
unusual_decisions.quote: Yesterday the System Office was hit with a number of malicious emails containing malware.

analyst_notes: A color-coded, sector/risk-based Continuity/Crisis Management Plan (six sectors), reviewed annually as part of the Strategic Plan and Policies & Procedures Manual. It organizes around likelihood-ordered risk sectors rather than essential-function inventories or time-based RTOs, assigning intervention/recovery strategies, escalating impact levels and points-of-contact per sector. Strong on risk-sector coverage, communications/call-trees, cyber incident response, NCCCS interdependencies, and an e-learning academic-continuity sector. Lacks promulgation authority, BIA, RTOs, delegations, alternate-facility strategy, vital-records management, devolution and an explicit reconstitution phase.

essential_functions: Mission-critical Information Resources and business functions identified in the Contingency Plan with associated contingency requirements; addresses maintaining essential mission and business functions despite disruption/compromise/failure
rto_tiers: Recovery objectives, restoration priorities and metrics provided in the Contingency Plan; recovery and reconstitution to a known state within the specified recovery time and recovery point objectives (RTO/RPO)
vital_records_approach: Information System Backup of system-level and critical user-level information plus system/security/privacy documentation; daily/weekly/monthly backups per system; daily backups copied to an offsite backup location at a geographically independent server room
tte_cadence: CIO tests, reassesses and maintains the Contingency Plan annually (virtual or simulated; an actual event satisfies the requirement); contingency training on assignment, on plan changes and periodically; lessons learned incorporated
reconstitution_treatment: Explicit 'Information System Recovery and Reconstitution' section: recovery and reconstitution of an Information System to a known state within specified RTO/RPO; eventual full Information System restoration without deterioration of controls
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (IT/Information-System scope; instructional continuity resides in the separately-maintained TWU COOP)
it_continuity_treatment: Entire policy is IT/Information-System contingency planning: alternate storage site, alternate telecommunications services, alternate communications protocols, information system backup, recovery and reconstitution, resilience (load-balancing, off-site replication, monitoring), aligned to NIST SP 800-53 CP controls
interdependencies_treatment: Defines Contractors/Vendors, governmental-agency employees and TWU-Affiliated institutions in scope; alternate telecommunications via necessary agreements; coordinates contingency with incident handling

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly maps its provisions to a named, partially-adopted NIST control set, disclosing the gaps in the state's adoption
unusual_decisions.quote: The State of Texas has chosen to adopt a select number of Contingency Planning (“CP”) principles established in NIST SP 800-53 “Contingency Planning” guidelines... so there are gaps in the numbering sequence.

unusual_decisions.decision: Counts an actual contingency activation as fulfilling the annual test requirement
unusual_decisions.quote: If an actual event occurs that requires that the Contingency Plan be executed, that event fulfills the annual test requirement.

unusual_decisions.decision: Mandates geographically independent, access-controlled server rooms with equivalent controls at the alternate site
unusual_decisions.quote: ITS shall maintain geographically independent server rooms where data is stored in a secure, environmentally safe, locked facility accessible only to TWU representatives.

analyst_notes: A NIST-aligned Information System Contingency Planning regulation (URP 04.775) that is explicitly the IT/DR companion to TWU's separately-maintained Continuity of Operations Plan (held by Risk Management under URP 04.410). It cites NIST SP 800-53 CP-1 through CP-11, 1 TAC Ch. 202 and the Texas DIR Security Standards Catalog, and covers mission-critical functions, RTO/RPO, alternate storage and telecommunications sites, backups, recovery and reconstitution, training and annual testing. As an IT-DR policy it omits a CONOPS narrative, BIA (deferred to the COOP), threat assessment, succession, delegations, activation triggers, devolution and academic continuity. Strong, doctrine-grounded IT contingency coverage.

essential_functions: (Defined, not inventoried) 'a definition of essential personnel and mission-critical functions' to be supplied by each department
succession_depth: Calls for unit policies on the emergency 'chain of command' but provides no orders-of-succession structure.
vital_records_approach: Preparedness guidance directs departments to back up cultures/data and keep offsite duplicates of critical data; recovery worksheet asks 'Who is responsible for backing up vital records/information? Where are copies of information stored?' - process prompts rather than a vital-records register.
tte_cadence: Directs that the plan be shared annually and that evacuation drills be conducted annually ('Conduct evacuation drills annually'); no broader continuity exercise program.
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery section covers support services/assistance, documenting outcomes, and detailed space/equipment/personnel impact assessments to drive program restoration; framed as recovery rather than formal reconstitution.
it_continuity_treatment: Addressed only as data/culture backup and offsite duplication within preparedness; no system contingency/IT DR plan.
interdependencies_treatment: States UMMC is 'an interdependent community' and that department plans support the institution's Emergency Operations Plan / NIMS-based Incident Command; no dependency mapping.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires managers to keep a copy of the plan at home and to brief all new personnel and re-share annually
unusual_decisions.quote: Keep multiple copies of the Plan in accessible locations throughout the department Ensure that managers keep a copy of the Plan at home

unusual_decisions.decision: Roots departmental continuity in NIMS and the Vice Chancellor's activation of the EOP/ICS
unusual_decisions.quote: During a major emergency or disaster, the Vice Chancellor and his/her designee will activate the Emergency Operations Plan and the Incident Command Structure will be utilized following the National Incident Management System guidelines.

analyst_notes: A medical-center departmental Disaster and Business Continuity Plan template organized into three phases - Preparedness, Response, Recovery - with checklists, team rosters, evacuation tools, emergency communications systems, and post-event impact assessment forms. It supports UMMC's institution-wide EOP/ICS (NIMS). Strong on personnel/team organization, communications, preparedness, response, and recovery, but as a phase-based template it lacks RTOs, a formal BIA, alternate continuity facilities, orders of succession, delegations of authority, devolution, and standards/budget detail. NIMS is referenced as the institutional ICS framework; coded as integrated departmental plan.

essential_functions: UT Police (building access controls, crowd control)
Office of Public Affairs Communications Team
Environmental Health & Safety and Risk Management (sanitation, hazardous waste, insurance assessments)
Center for Laboratory Animal Medicine and Care (animal care)
Auxiliary Enterprises (housing, parking, mail, shuttle, dining, bookstore)
rto_tiers: IT Disaster Recovery RTO under 48 hours for ~30 critical systems, RPO ranging from minutes to hours; non-critical systems vary with some rebuilds up to 30 days; plan governs the 30-day post-event window
vital_records_approach: Data protection via network/offsite backups and a 'Protection of Data' worksheet (network-drive saves, physically separate backups, recovery of locally retained data); asset documentation for insurance recovery; IT-DRP replicates critical systems to secondary datacenters (Guhn Rd, Arlington) or offsite backup
tte_cadence: IT-DR maintenance schedule lists annual 'Critical system DR/end-user testing' and 'Update testing schedule' tasks with scheduled reviews; no broader BCP exercise program (tabletop/functional), training program, or after-action/improvement cycle for the BCP itself
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct return-to-normal process; plan scope is limited to the ~30-day post-event recovery window
academic_continuity_treatment: Limited to academic-support continuity: Registrar's Office tracks courses/grades for fulfillment of academic requirements and processes new applicants; Canvas LMS and academic systems are in the IT-DRP; no instructional pivot-to-remote plan
it_continuity_treatment: Detailed standalone IT Disaster Recovery Plan for ~30 critical systems (PeopleSoft FMS/HRMS, COUPA, Epic EHR, Canvas) using ASP-contracted DR, secondary-datacenter replication, or offsite backup/rebuild; RTO under 48h, RPO minutes-to-hours; dedicated IT Disaster Recovery Team feeds status to Public Affairs
interdependencies_treatment: Operational entities explicitly rely on the eleven institutional infrastructure services; functional-process tables show affected systems/impacts; critical-vendor and peer-to-peer (in/out of region) support identified in worksheets; financial transactions dependent on IT-infrastructure status

unusual_decisions.decision: Scopes the entire plan to the 30-day post-event window, justified by lessons from Tropical Storm Allison
unusual_decisions.quote: Based upon the experience with Tropical Storm Allison, 30 days post event is the most critical timeframe with regard to immediate recovery decisions, resource procurement, and the establishment of temporary means and locations of essential functions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds the support-services BCP as paper-form 'Disaster Recovery Mode' workflows for procurement, cash, HR, payroll and asset management to run when information systems are down
unusual_decisions.quote: In the absence of institution-wide information system access, the tables following on pages 8-12 provide an overview of the processes related to procurement, capital asset management, cash management, human resources/payroll, and Auxiliary Enterprises parking.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates a color-coded (green/yellow/red) building-and-operational status updated twice daily during impairment
unusual_decisions.quote: This form is to be completed by the authorized facility/operations coordinator while a facility remains in a yellow or red status and/or operations are impaired.

analyst_notes: Read all 73 pages. This is UTHealth Houston's 'Business Continuity Plan - Infrastructure (Support Services),' a March 2025 companion to the Emergency Management Plan and IT Disaster Recovery Plan; the latter half (Appendices I-III) is operational flowcharts, disaster-preparedness/building-status worksheets, and manual finance/HR/equipment forms (the form fields seen on download). It is strong on essential infrastructure functions, business-process analysis, IT-DR with RTO/RPO, interdependencies, communications, vital-data protection, version control, and an IT testing schedule. As a deliberately narrow support-services companion document it omits a signed promulgation, a continuity CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations (only a worksheet 'adapted from ready.gov'), a threat/hazard assessment (deferred to the EMP), budgeting, orders of succession, delegations of authority, devolution, and a reconstitution/return-to-normal process.

essential_functions: (Defined as a concept, not inventoried) 'The critical activities performed by organizations, especially after a disruption of normal activities' - examples given: ensuring public safety, maintenance/repair of infrastructure, conducting damage assessments, identifying/leasing alternative facilities, funding disaster recovery
succession_depth: Defines 'Orders of Succession - Provisions for the assumption of senior university offices during an emergency in the event that any of those officials are unavailable to execute their duties' as a required element; not a populated list.
vital_records_approach: Defines 'Vital Records Management - the identification, protection and ready availability of electronic and hard copy documents, references, records, information systems, data management...' as a required element.
tte_cadence: States the COOP 'should be tested, at a minimum, once a year' as a general principle, but no exercise program/types are defined.
academic_continuity_treatment: Mentions continuity of student courses as a planning question ('Can we ensure accountability of students and continue student courses during a disruption?') but provides no instructional-continuity procedures.
it_continuity_treatment: Treated within continuity-facilities/communications and vital-records definitions (information systems, data management); no standalone IT DR section.

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly warns planners not to assume the normal management hierarchy will be available and to empower alternates
unusual_decisions.quote: It is important to remember that the normal decision-makers for a department may not be available in an emergency. It can be important to designate alternate decision-makers and ensure they are empowered to take action if necessary.

unusual_decisions.decision: Recommends scenario-agnostic (impact-based) planning rather than hazard-by-hazard planning
unusual_decisions.quote: Planning for specific scenarios - For example, instead of planning for what to do in a flood, fire, etc., plan for what to do if your normal building was inaccessible for any reason.

analyst_notes: A 2-page COOP 'overview' / planning-guidance document that defines what a COOP is, key planning principles, and walks through the FCD-style 'Elements of a Viable Continuity of Operations Plan' (essential functions, orders of succession, delegations of authority, continuity facilities incl. telework, continuity communications, vital records management). It is guidance for writing plans rather than a populated plan, so it presents the element keys conceptually but lacks an actual essential-functions inventory, RTOs, activation triggers, dependencies, devolution/reconstitution, and a TT&E program (annual testing is stated as a principle only).

essential_functions: Critical district services and critical application servers (Banner System and interfacing third-party apps, e-mail, Internet, Intranet, file sharing, network authentication, DNS, DHCP, network management); critical computing capabilities to be restored in an orderly course of action
vital_records_approach: Banner servers backed up to tape on full/partial schedule with tapes rotated offsite weekly, plus backup to Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud for double redundancy; 365-day minimum retention for sensitive data; data on servers/SANs in scope
tte_cadence: No DR plan-level training/exercise/after-action program (equipment-level checks only: generator tested monthly/weekly, fire-suppression annual testing, UPS self-tests, test/replica databases)
reconstitution_treatment: Documents disaster recovery/emergency activation, execution and reconstitution procedures; defines a reconstitution mechanism to get business back to normal from a disaster-recovery state; reconstitution relies on maintenance agreements for technical expertise and spare components
academic_continuity_treatment: Context only: notes students rely on ITS to register, conduct online instruction and communicate with faculty and that 24x7 availability became business-critical with COVID-19 remote work/learning; no instructional-continuity provisions (academic continuity is not the plan's purpose)
it_continuity_treatment: Full operational ITS Disaster Recovery Plan: IT/network infrastructure inventory across Chabot/Las Positas/EDCE/Dublin, hardware/software redundancy, backup systems, UPS/generator power and cooling resilience, fire suppression, two service-interruption levels (predictable vs. exceptional failures), DR initiation/detection, execution and reconstitution, plus detailed server/database recovery appendices
interdependencies_treatment: Dependencies on service providers to repair their infrastructure after an earthquake; maintenance agreements with hardware/software/infrastructure vendors for spare components and expertise; insurance carriers consulted on repair-vs-temporary-facilities decisions; Banner/third-party application interfaces

unusual_decisions.decision: Bounds the plan's ambition explicitly, declining to plan for district-wide catastrophe while still guiding central-computing restoration
unusual_decisions.quote: The Disaster Recovery plan is not designed to address conditions of widespread damage throughout the District.

unusual_decisions.decision: Pairs weekly offsite tape rotation with AWS cloud backup to achieve deliberate double redundancy for the Banner system
unusual_decisions.quote: In addition to the tape backup, the systems are backed up to Amazon Web Services (AWS) in the cloud. This provides for double redundancy in the even that a backup need to be access for file recovery.

unusual_decisions.decision: Routes the repair-versus-relocate decision through ITS management together with the District's insurance carriers
unusual_decisions.quote: ITS management and other District management would work with the District’s insurance carriers to determine whether to pursue repair or to secure temporary facilities.

analyst_notes: A 34-page operational ITS Disaster Recovery Plan (April 2021, submitted by the CTO) for a multi-college district, organized around response/recovery/resumption/restoration/return phases plus DR initiation and reconstitution. It is detailed on IT and facility infrastructure, redundancy (hardware, UPS/generator, cooling, fire suppression), offsite+AWS backups, threat/risk profiling (earthquake/fire/computer crime), alternate/temporary facilities and a reconstitution mechanism backed by vendor maintenance agreements. Because it is IT-DR scoped and authored as a submitted technical document, it lacks a promulgation/approval grant, named doctrine, a plan-maintenance cadence, BIA, RTO/RPO, succession, delegations, devolution, academic-continuity provisions and a DR-exercise/training program.

essential_functions: Essential business functions identified and prioritized per unit via the BIA (no enumerated university-wide list); units identify, document, and prioritize their key business functions
rto_tiers: BIA establishes 'maximum allowable downtime and recovery time objectives (RTOs) for each function' and 'acceptable level of losses and recovery point objectives (RPOs)'; no fixed tier scheme
tte_cadence: Annual BCP review and annual training/refresh (new staff trained annually, confirmed in a Quality Assurance Review); every two years each unit conducts a tabletop exercise/drill with a documented, retained exercise assessment
reconstitution_treatment: Stated as a BCP aim ('efficiently return to normal operations') but no reconstitution process described
academic_continuity_treatment: Requires faculty inclusion per the Faculty Handbook and applies to faculty/academic officers, but no instructional-continuity, pivot-to-remote, or academic-calendar provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Explicitly out of scope: disaster recovery plans developed by Information Systems and Facility Operations are excluded from this policy
interdependencies_treatment: BIA requires units to 'identify, document, and communicate interdependencies with other units or among functions within a unit' and to map critical paths/dependencies for restoring functions

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly carves IT disaster recovery, emergency response, and crisis communications out of the BCM policy's scope
unusual_decisions.quote: Out of the scope of this policy are: Emergency response plans (defined by the Emergency Procedures and Communications Policy), Crisis management and crisis communications plans, and Disaster recovery plans (developed by Information Systems and Facility Operations).

unusual_decisions.decision: Mandates a structured six-step BCM process (BIA, risk assessment, risk-reduction strategies, BCP development, training, biennial evaluation) for every unit
unusual_decisions.quote: Units must complete a six-step process and annually review each step to ensure currency of plans and the unit's ability to execute the plans.

unusual_decisions.decision: Stores confidential unit BCPs in access-controlled repositories (DePaul Knowledgebase wiki or OneDrive) chosen by the unit
unusual_decisions.quote: Since BCPs may contain information about DePaul's internal business processes that is confidential in nature the repository must also have access control.

analyst_notes: Read all 6 pages. DePaul's Business Continuity Management Policy (effective 2021, originated 2017) is a unit-level program/policy: a responsible-officer-owned policy mandating a six-step process per unit (BIA with RTO/RPO/MTD and interdependencies, risk assessment, risk-reduction strategies, a BCP that includes succession plans and facility alternatives, annual training, biennial tabletop exercises), with access-controlled plan repositories and an annual review cycle. It is deliberately subservient to the Emergency Procedures policy and EOP, and it expressly excludes IT disaster recovery and crisis communications, so it omits a CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, delegations of authority, resilient continuity communications, a vital-records program, activation triggers, devolution, a reconstitution process, IT-DR, and instructional continuity. Orders of succession are present because the required BCP must include 'succession plans for individuals in leadership role or with specialized expertise.'

essential_functions: (Operational-area based, not a formal MEF list) Academic Affairs / Continuity of Instruction; Research Facilities; Enrollment & Registration; Human Resources; Information Technology Services; Facility Management & Operations; Athletics; Transportation; Housing/Residential
reconstitution_treatment: Document is a recovery-and-continuity plan for resuming/sustaining campus operations under COVID-19 (move-in and re-entry testing, return-to-work, phased resumption of services); functions as recovery/return-to-operations rather than formal post-disaster reconstitution.
academic_continuity_treatment: 'Plan for Continuity of Instruction': every course maintains a Blackboard presence with an alternative syllabus; instructional delivery modes determined per course (face-to-face, internship/practicum/clinical/lab, law clinics); enrollment/registration, teaching laboratories, and research facilities addressed; explicit commitment that neither instructional effectiveness nor safety is compromised.
it_continuity_treatment: Information Technology Services section provides loaner equipment (PCs/Apple laptops for faculty/staff; Chromebooks for students) and electronic records/reporting systems (Medicat, Veoci); supports remote work/instruction but is not an enterprise IT DR plan.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats compliance with public-health community standards as a condition of employment with a defined progressive-discipline ladder ending in termination
unusual_decisions.quote: First offense - Supervisor will counsel employee2. Second offense - Written warning3. Third offense - 10-day suspension without pay4. Fourth offense will result in termination

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly defers detailed continuity elements to a separate companion plan
unusual_decisions.quote: Specific planning elements and policies are documented in the NCCU Pandemic and Communicable Disease Emergency Response Plan which is available upon request from ehs@nccu.edu.

analyst_notes: NCCU's Fall 2021 COVID-19 Operations, Recovery and Continuity Plan: a 39-page pandemic operations document spanning community health standards, testing/quarantine, events, travel, HR, athletics, IT services, facilities, plus a 'Plan for Continuity of Instruction' and research-facilities operations. It is organized departmentally/by operational area rather than around mission-essential functions, and explicitly points to a separate Pandemic and Communicable Disease Emergency Response Plan for detailed planning elements. Strong on continuity of instruction, activation context (CDC/UNC System guidance), communications, personnel/HR, risk (pandemic) and reconstitution-as-return-to-operations; lacks RTOs, a BIA, succession/delegations, alternate continuity facilities, vital records, devolution, and formal continuity-standards citations.

essential_functions: Oversight of human research / IRB review (across IRB-HSBS, IRBMED, ORCR, Minors in Research Compliance Monitoring, HPSCRO Committee, and Coordinated Services and Practices)
succession_depth: Lists department leadership and emergency contacts and designates lead emergency coordinators/planners (HRPP Director and Associate Director), but no ordered line of succession for assuming authority.
academic_continuity_treatment: Research-continuity focus: criteria for which human-subjects studies may continue, be newly implemented, postponed, or halted during an emergency; continued/remote IRB review (remote/teleconference meetings, ad hoc meetings, consent/documentation waivers, alternate safety-monitoring mechanisms), reliance on external IRBs per master agreements, and requesting relief from federal deadlines.
it_continuity_treatment: Identifies eResearch as U-M's electronic research administration system (eResearch Regulatory Management) as an essential resource; communications fall back to phone/video/email/in-person; no separate IT DR plan.
interdependencies_treatment: Maps reliance on OVPR coordination, external IRBs under master agreements, federal regulatory agencies, and U-M central systems (eResearch); internal/external/research-community communication plans identify dependent stakeholders and distribution lists.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines explicit clinical-risk criteria for which active studies may continue during a disruption
unusual_decisions.quote: If the study is active, discontinuing the study would present a high degree of risk to participants(e.g., implanted device)

unusual_decisions.decision: Plans proactively to seek regulatory relief and lean on external IRBs to sustain oversight
unusual_decisions.quote: Contact federal agencies to request relief from deadline requirements, as appropriate.

analyst_notes: A current (May 2025) department-level COOP for the U-M Human Research Protection Program, framed within the institutional expectation that all U-M units maintain plans covering essential functions, staffing, supplies, contract services, communications, travel, and contingency planning. Essential-functions-based and notably strong on research continuity (study triage, remote IRB operations, regulatory-relief and external-IRB strategies), communications, personnel work-redesign/redeployment, interdependencies, activation (Regents' SPG 201.27 campus-closure trigger), and annual review/maintenance. As a small administrative-unit COOP it omits RTOs, BIA, alternate physical facilities, vital records, succession/delegations, devolution, reconstitution, and a TT&E program.

essential_functions: University Enterprises and Events (Campus Dining, St. Michael's Laundry, Cedar Grove)
rto_tiers: Template requires setting 'Minimum operational levels and recovery time frames' and a 'Maximum Time to go without' the essential function; no institution-wide tier scheme
tte_cadence: Each required area reviews its plan and exercises it at least annually (tabletop or part of the larger University annual drill); training of affected personnel is documented; RMS coordinates the University's annual drill and reports gaps
reconstitution_treatment: Framed as the program's overall purpose ('managing the University's return to an operational status after an incident') but no reconstitution process is described
academic_continuity_treatment: Guiding principle of 'Continuity of student learning, teaching and research'; ND Research (Provost) is a required-plan department; no instructional pivot or academic-calendar provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Office of Information Technologies is on the required-plan list and the template asks units to list 'IT Infrastructure Requirements,' but no IT disaster-recovery program, backups, or alternate processing is established
interdependencies_treatment: Template requires documenting dependencies (power, IT, people), suppliers/contractors with MOUs or open POs, and an optional table of internal/external notification and 'response to get infrastructure back online'

unusual_decisions.decision: Directs that any existing policy conflicting with disruption needs be amended rather than worked around
unusual_decisions.quote: Amending existing policies that require provisions for specific recovery situations.

unusual_decisions.decision: Provides a worked template example (campus dining producing 7,500 meals/day, fire protection) showing infrastructure, dependencies, max time without, and get-back-online actions
unusual_decisions.quote: Produce at least 7,500 meals per day ... 2 days ... Buy food from another company - list vendor

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes storage of all departmental plans with Risk Management and Safety while units also retain their own copies
unusual_decisions.quote: Risk Management and Safety shall maintain copies of the business continuity plans for each division and department required to maintain/develop one.

analyst_notes: Read all 7 pages. Notre Dame's Business Continuity Program (SAFE028, August 2021) is a short framework/policy document plus a BCP template: it names owners and an approval date, requires 13 listed departments to build/maintain/test plans annually, defines mission-critical functions, and supplies a template covering essential functions, dependencies/suppliers (MOUs), recovery time frames, a communications plan, and outsource options. It deliberately focuses 'on critical functions rather than incident causation,' so it omits a CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, a threat/risk assessment, succession, delegations of authority, a vital-records program, activation triggers, devolution, a reconstitution process, and an IT disaster-recovery program.

tte_cadence: At least annual review; plan testing plus training and exercises occur in Phase I (Readiness & Preparedness).
reconstitution_treatment: Phase IV Reconstitution: return to original facility/staffing, relocate long-term if needed, and review how well the plan worked using team feedback.

unusual_decisions.decision: Organizes the entire unit-COOP program explicitly around the four FEMA continuity phases.
unusual_decisions.quote: There are four phases of the continuity process, beginning with developing your COOP and training to stay prepared.

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly equates COOP and Business Continuity Plan terminology for its campus audience.
unusual_decisions.quote: A Continuity of Operations Plan (COOP), also known as a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) is a collection of resources, actions, procedures, and information that is developed, tested and kept ready in the event of a disruption of operations.

analyst_notes: Read in full (HTML program page). App State's Emergency Preparedness office runs a phase-based COOP program that pushes operational planning down to departments via a Quick Start Guide, so coop_treatment is 'program'. The page thoroughly describes the four phases, essential-function prioritization, alternate/telework operations, activation, and reconstitution, but as a program landing page it does not itself contain a BIA, RTOs, succession/delegation tables, or vital-records detail — those live in the unit plans.

essential_functions: Departments providing services essential to the mission (determined by the Business Continuity Steering Committee); each department lists its own essential functions/critical processes in Chico Ready
rto_tiers: Function impact/criticality ratings: 1 = Must Continue (Life, Health, Security); 2 = Must Continue in reduced mode; 3 = Pause if forced but must resume within 30 days; D = Deferrable; NA = Not applicable
tte_cadence: Per EO 1014: a portion of each plan tested every year, with the entire plan tested once every 7 years; Risk Management provides training to each identified department
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (generic essential-services framing; no instructional continuity)
it_continuity_treatment: Not addressed (planning scenarios include operating without electricity/staffing/facility, but no IT disaster recovery)
interdependencies_treatment: Worksheet flags impact on Vendors and mitigation of loss of power/facility, but no dependency mapping

unusual_decisions.decision: Delivers the entire continuity capability through a commercial planning platform (Kuali/Chico Ready) with step-by-step department self-service
unusual_decisions.quote: The software program, Chico Ready, provides step by step instructions to assist each user in planning for continuity of operations without Electricity, Adequate Staffing or Facility/Building.

unusual_decisions.decision: Codifies a tiered testing obligation (annual partial / 7-year full) drawn from the CSU executive order
unusual_decisions.quote: EO 1014 requires that a portion of each plan is tested every year, with the entire plan being tested once every 7 years.

unusual_decisions.decision: Requires a physical signed cover page routed to Risk Management to formalize each departmental plan
unusual_decisions.quote: The Department head or designee will need to: 1. Print a copy of the plan 2. Sign and date the first page of the printed plan. 3. Forward a copy of the signed cover page to Risk Management.

analyst_notes: A short program-overview / how-to deck (22 slides) for CSU Chico's Business Continuity Program, framed entirely around CSU Executive Order 1014's seven program elements and executed through the Kuali 'Chico Ready' software. It is a programmatic framework, not an operational plan, so it covers governance well (EO 1014 authority, BIA/Risk worksheet with criticality tiers, departmental essential functions, training, plan review/renewal, and tiered testing) but omits operational COOP elements such as CONOPS, succession, delegations, alternate facilities, continuity communications, vital records, activation, devolution, reconstitution, IT-DR and academic continuity, which would reside in individual departmental plans.

essential_functions: Critical functions identified and documented by each Unit and categorized by OEM-established levels of criticality (ranking/ordering standard)
rto_tiers: OEM establishes a standard for ranking/ordering critical functions by criticality; strategies for prioritizing, maintaining and/or resuming functions; no RTO/RPO/MTD times
tte_cadence: Each Unit conducts and documents at least one continuity exercise annually with after-action review and lessons learned (a real-world interruption may substitute); OEM conducts training for Continuity Plan Owners and Managers; Crisis Management Team participates in annual exercises
reconstitution_treatment: Recovery strategies/plans and 'resuming those functions' referenced in definitions, but no reconstitution/return-to-normal process
academic_continuity_treatment: Schools and research institutes are scoped as 'Units' that must plan, but no instructional-continuity provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Policy requires Units to develop, maintain and exercise business continuity AND disaster recovery plans; no IT-DR technical detail (set by OEM standard system)
interdependencies_treatment: Mitigation strategies for each critical function must consider 'loss of infrastructure, loss of personnel, loss of financial resources, and dependencies'

unusual_decisions.decision: Lets a documented real-world interruption with an after-action review substitute for the required annual continuity exercise
unusual_decisions.quote: In lieu of an exercise, Units may use real-word incidents that caused an interruption to the Unit’s critical functions. Units should still document the incident with an after-action review and incorporate appropriate lessons learned into their continuity plan.

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes a single criticality-ranking standard at OEM that every Unit must use, enforcing comparability across the institute
unusual_decisions.quote: OEM shall establish a standard for ranking/ordering critical functions based on criticality and all Units shall use this same standard when identifying their critical functions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Makes the Crisis Management Team responsible for forcing resource allocation to bring lapsed unit plans into compliance
unusual_decisions.quote: Ensures that Units with expired plans or with significant planning gaps prioritize the review of their plans and allocate appropriate resources to bring their plans into compliance.

analyst_notes: A concise governance policy (approved by the Policy Steering Committee, October 2023) that sets institute-wide requirements for unit-level business continuity and disaster recovery planning rather than operational procedures. It mandates identification/criticality-ranking of critical functions, mitigation strategies considering dependencies, designated Continuity Plan Managers, annual exercises with after-action reviews, and annual maintenance under OEM oversight, with leadership resourcing. As a requirements policy it omits a CONOPS, named doctrine, BIA, RTOs, risk assessment, succession, delegations, alternate facilities, continuity communications, vital records, activation triggers, devolution, reconstitution and academic continuity, all of which are pushed down to the unit plans the policy requires.

rto_tiers: Time-graduated instructional response tiers: disruption lasting 1-5 days (college-wide + instructor communication); 6-10 days (add delivery of online content via email/Blackboard/faculty web pages/alternate CMS); 10+ days (administration determines viability of the semester and delivery mode).
academic_continuity_treatment: Faculty quick reference for the Continuity of Academic Operations Plan (CAOP): syllabus CAOP statement, use of the LMS (auto-created course sites per class), tiered continuity-of-instruction timelines, and behavior when internet is/is not available; CAOP can change syllabi, assignments, assessment, evaluation, and grades.
it_continuity_treatment: Relies on the LMS, IVCC website, email and automated phone messaging for academic continuity; CETLA support to ensure faculty can access LMS tools; no enterprise IT DR.

unusual_decisions.decision: Activation of academic continuity is strictly subordinate to a formal Emergency Response Plan activation
unusual_decisions.quote: The CAOP is activated only after college officials have declared an emergency and activated the college's Emergency Response Plan (ERP). The CAOP will not be activated without an official ERP activation.

unusual_decisions.decision: Sets a hard threshold at which the institution decides whether the semester is salvageable
unusual_decisions.quote: Should a disruption last 10 or more days, IVCC administration will determine the viability of the semester and classes delivered in any mode.

analyst_notes: A 2-page faculty quick reference to IVCC's Continuity of Academic Operations Plan (CAOP), explicitly activated only after the college's Emergency Response Plan is activated (clear activation trigger / decision authority). Strong, distinctive feature is the day-count tiered instructional-response model (1-5 / 6-10 / 10+ days) functioning as a priority/recovery-tier scheme, plus LMS-based delivery and resilient communications. As an academic-continuity document it legitimately omits essential-functions inventory, BIA, threat assessment, succession, facilities, vital records, dedicated continuity team, devolution, IT DR, and a TT&E program.

reconstitution_treatment: Section 10 'Recovery and Resumption of On-Campus Instruction' describes a gradual return to in-person instruction with revised course structures, extended timelines, and student support services to help students catch up.
academic_continuity_treatment: Core of the document: transition to online/remote learning via Canvas and Zoom, alternative assignments and assessments, flexible deadlines, equitable technology access (laptops, tablets, Wi-Fi hotspots), continued academic/student services (tutoring, library, advising), backup plans for technology failures, and faculty training/support; grounded in CA Education Code (§70902(b), §84810), Title 5 (§55002, §55200), and ACCJC instructional-hour standards.
it_continuity_treatment: Online platforms (Canvas/Zoom) plus backup options for technology failures (phone-based learning, email-based assignments, faculty tech support); no enterprise IT DR.

unusual_decisions.decision: Anchors instructional continuity to specific California Education Code / Title 5 instructional-hour and emergency-adjustment provisions to protect state apportionment
unusual_decisions.quote: Lost instructional hours risk loss of state funding (apportionment) unless alternatives (e.g., online instruction) are used to meet the minimum hours.

unusual_decisions.decision: Makes equitable technology access an explicit continuity requirement
unusual_decisions.quote: Colleges must ensure that all students, especially those lacking resources for remote learning, have access to technology such as laptops, tablets, and Wi-Fi hotspots.

analyst_notes: A DRAFT (1.16.2025) academic/instructional-continuity plan focused entirely on keeping instruction running during emergencies: remote-learning transition, alternative assignments/assessments, equitable access, communication channels, technology-failure backups, faculty support, and recovery/resumption of on-campus instruction. Standards alignment is present but it is academic-regulatory (CA Ed Code, Title 5, ACCJC) rather than continuity standards (FCD/NIST/ISO). As an academic-continuity plan it is strong on instruction and recovery but legitimately omits essential-functions inventory, BIA, RTOs, threat assessment, succession, facilities, vital records, dedicated continuity personnel/team, and a TT&E program.

academic_continuity_treatment: Detailed instructional-continuity guidance: campus-closure communication, OAA email to faculty, required syllabus emergency-preparedness statement, and a menu of options (new independent asynchronous assignment, asynchronous recorded Zoom session, synchronous LSU virtual platform, incompletes, adjusting due dates); explicit handling of labs and online students elsewhere in the world; continuation of research mentioned; tied to PS 45 (engaged learning time) and PS 117 (make-up solution).
it_continuity_treatment: Uses Moodle/Zoom/Teams for instruction and accessibility tooling (Ally captioning); notes power/internet outages may impair online delivery and trigger PS 117 makeup days; no enterprise IT DR.

unusual_decisions.decision: Primary stated objective is to avoid weekend/break make-up classes by pivoting modality instead of cancelling
unusual_decisions.quote: An important objective is to avoid the necessity for make-up classes on weekends or during planned breaks.

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds disability accommodations directly into the modality-shift plan, noting machine captioning is insufficient for compliance
unusual_decisions.quote: Please note that machine captioning does not meet the threshold for accessibility compliance. For live (synchronous) meetings, there may be need for live captioning or ASL interpreter (based upon student accommodation needs).

analyst_notes: A focused academic-continuity plan (Office of Academic Affairs) covering continuation of LSU's academic mission during campus closures: communication flow, contingency-planning expectations for faculty, a syllabus statement template, concrete instructional-delivery options, and a substantial accessibility/accommodations section. Standards alignment is to LSU policy statements (PS 45/PS 117) governing instructional time and make-up, which counts. As an academic-continuity plan it intentionally omits essential-functions inventory, BIA, RTOs, succession, facilities, vital records, dedicated continuity team, devolution/reconstitution, IT DR, and a TT&E program.

essential_functions: Health and safety of students, faculty, staff, and visitors
Delivery of teaching/learning and other student-related services
Continuation of research and maintenance of research infrastructure
Security and preservation of University facilities and equipment
Maintenance of support for partnerships with the community
succession_depth: Critical Operations table provides only an 'Alternate Person #1' column per essential operation; no leadership orders of succession.
academic_continuity_treatment: Listed only at the institutional-priority level ('Delivery of teaching/learning and other student-related services'; 'Continuation of research and maintenance of research infrastructure'); no instructional-continuity procedures.
interdependencies_treatment: Resources/Supplies and Services tables prompt for current and alternate internal/external suppliers and service providers, but no dependency mapping is completed in the template.

unusual_decisions.decision: States the system-agnostic continuity design principle as a header on the Critical Operations page
unusual_decisions.quote: Department plans should be prepared with the goal of enabling the University's essential functions to continue regardless of whether or not systems are operational, facilities and infrastructure services are available, or other organizations are viable.

analyst_notes: A short (6-page) fill-in departmental BCP template from EHS, organized around critical/essential operations in order of importance with responsible person and alternate, a high-priority readiness checklist, and essential resources/supplies/services tables. It is almost entirely blank fields, so most program elements (RTO, vital records, IT DR, succession, activation triggers, maintenance) are not substantively present; it carries an institutional-priorities statement and a department-chair approval signature block. Coded honestly as a thin template.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines a fixed 3-month, 7-step department onboarding workflow culminating in submission into a records system (Laserfiche)
unusual_decisions.quote: Submit the completed continuity plan to the Business Continuity Program Coordinator. The Office of Safety and Security will review your continuity plan and give you the go-ahead to have your plan signed by your department head.

analyst_notes: This is a single-page 'Continuity Planning Quick Start Guide,' not a continuity plan itself - it describes the institutional program process for departments (designate a liaison, attend a workshop, draft with stakeholders, route for review/approval, submit to Laserfiche). It evidences a program with required review, department-head approval (promulgation), and a coordinator role, but contains none of the substantive plan components. Coded as coop_treatment=program; the only present benchmarks are the program/promulgation and plan-maintenance-via-review references plus the definition of a continuity plan; nearly all element keys are missing because the document is a process guide.

rto_tiers: Generic guidance only ('full operational capability for essential functions no later than 12 hours after activation... sustain operations for up to 30 days, 60 days during a Pandemic'); no institution-specific RTO/RPO/MTD
vital_records_approach: Names 'Essential Records' as one of the 10 elements but provides no TTU-System vital-records identification, protection, or access provisions
tte_cadence: Advocates TT&E generically as Program Phase 5 and a planning consideration ('regularly scheduled testing, training & exercising'); no TTU-System cadence, exercise types, or after-action process
devolution_treatment: Named as element 8 ('Devolution of control') with no TTU-System provisions
reconstitution_treatment: Named as element 9 and Phase 7 ('Implementation of reconstitution and devolution if necessary') with no TTU-System provisions
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed beyond listing Provost/CAO as example COOP-team contributors
it_continuity_treatment: Not addressed
interdependencies_treatment: Not addressed (BPA named as a Phase-2 activity but no dependency mapping)
analyst_notes: Read all 15 slides. This artifact is not an operational continuity plan: it is a 2017 PowerPoint 'Continuity of Operations (COOP) Program Overview' by Kyle Gunn, Assistant Director, Office of Risk Management, that teaches what a COOP is. It enumerates the 10 FEMA/FCD continuity elements and a 7-phase program-development model and gives a Wisconsin-fire activation example, but contains no Texas Tech University System-specific provisions for any element. I therefore code only the program concept of operations (the lifecycle/program model) as present and list the remaining 21 components as missing because they are described generically rather than established for the institution. Data-quality flag: the source document_type ('full-coop') does not match this awareness/training deck.
